EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major squall line with multiple supercell thunderstorms moved across north and central Florida. While no tornado touchdowns occurred in the Jacksonville area of responsibility several significant touchdowns occurred just south of Marion and Flagler Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and poor drainage contributed to flooding in the Flagler Estates neighborhood in St. Johns County. Several roads were flooded with at least one car in a ditch following the flooding. Some portions of the roadway were washed out. Source 2/3/2007 St. Augustine Record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado of EF0 intensity occurred in association with a discreet thunderstorm cell with attended radar-observed shallow mesocyclone at Sugarloaf Key.  This event occurred within a large stratiform rain shield associated with a tropical disturbance which was later classified as Tropical Storm Barry in the eastern Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down at 1155 EST June 1st along Mira Road on Sugarloaf Key, downing a 30-foot Ficus tree, damaging a screen porch and scattering loose items stored underneath an elevated home.  The tornado likely had an intermittent north northwest path partially over water, before damging a home on Hawksbill Road along the northeast portion of Sugarloaf Key.  An enclosed patio along the back portion of the home had been destroyed, allowing metal covering to peel back on roughly one third of the home. No other homes were damaged, including one across a canal directly in the path of the tornado. Therefore it was concluded the the roof covering failure without any wood roof decking failures occurred due to the failure of the light wood porch.  Heavy rain was noted which also may have caused excessive loads on the screens, contributing to porch failure.  Therefore the maximum damage rating for the entire event was set at EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved south out of Miami-Dade County on the mainland, crossed the east end of Florida Bay, and produced sporadic wind damage throughout North Key Largo, Key Largo and Rock Harbor.

River Road was closed due to thick smoke and flames near the roadway. Fire continued to smolder into the following week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning initiated three fires in the vicinity of Camp Bland (Clay County), Florida on Monday afternoon, May 7th.  Strong winds quickly combined those fires into a single blaze with over 5,000 acres burning by Monday night.  The fire area extended from the Starke area to near Waldo in northeastern Alachua County.  By Tuesday 700 homes were evacuated with over 1,000 persons displaced from their homes. State Road 100 was closed from Keystone Heights to Starke and State Road 18 is closed from Hampton to State Road 100.  fanned by high winds and low relative humidity values over the next few days the fire continued to grow eventually reaching 14,626 acres. The fire remains active in the swamp without significant threat to structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning initiated three fires in the vicinity of Camp Bland (Clay County), Florida on Monday afternoon, May 7th.  Strong winds quickly combined those fires into a single blaze with over 5,000 acres burning by Monday night.  The fire area extended from the Starke area to near Waldo in northeastern Alachua County.  By Tuesday 700 homes were evacuated with over 1,000 persons displaced from their homes. State Road 100 was closed from Keystone Heights to Starke and State Road 18 is closed from Hampton to State Road 100.  Fanned by high winds and low relative humidity values over the next few days the fire continued to grow eventually reaching 14,626 acres. The fire remains active in the swamp without significant threat to structures through the month. As of July 28th the Florida Division of Forestry still had this fire open as an active fire due to the possibility of it reigniting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes on Saturday night, May 5th, ignited two 11 acre and 21 acre fires on Bugaboo Island in the southern Okefenokee National Wildlife Refuge (southwest of Stephen Foster State Park).  On the 8th through 10th high winds around large coastal low pressure system quickly resulted in extreme fire spread in southern Ware and Clinch counties in Southeast Georgia (The Georgia Bugaboo Scrub Fire). The fires combined and raced southward toward Baker and Columbia counties in north Florida becoming the Florida Bugaboo Scrub Fire. Due to close proximity to large populated areas, most significantly Lake City, Florida the fire generated large media attention and became the dominant fire between the two Bugaboo Scrub complexes.  By late on the 8th Georgia State Road 94 and Florida State Road 2 (All one road with two designations) was closed from Moniac, Georgia to Fargo, Georgia due to the threat of the Bugaboo Scrub fire crossing SR 2 and poor visibility because of smoke in the area. By 4:00 p.m. EDT 500 people were evacuated from the vicinity of Taylor, Florida in northern Baker County and others were evacuated near Moniac, Georgia. The focus the fire fighting efforts from May 8th-10th was on structure protection in the Taylor and Baxter, Florida area.  Fire crews, along with private timber companies, were working along the flanks of the fire where conditions allowed, as well as, working to protect structures.  Fire crews and forest industry personnel were also strengthening lines on the southwest edge of the Georgia Bugaboo Scrub near Fargo. The Southern Area Blue Team assumed responsibility of the Florida Bugaboo Scrub Fire, South of State Road 2, on May 10th at 10:00 a.m. EDT. At this point the fire had burned nearly 26,746 acres.||From the 10th through the 13th the fire burned southwest toward Deep Creek and lake City in Columbia County causing evacuations in those areas and sporadic closings of Interstates 10 and 75 in the area. By the 13th the Florida Bugaboo Scrub Fire had burned 102,000 acres, Georgia Bugaboo had burned 131,718 acres and the totals for the two were 233, 718 acres and 570 persons were forced from their homes. Smoke from these large fires produced hazy conditions as far south as Miami over the weekend.  On May 14th, About 4:00 p.m. the fire jumped containment line in the Fairview Road area east of US-441, about five miles north of the Deep Creek community but was contained overnight.  At this time the fire was considered to be 50 percent contained.  Tuesday, May 15th became a critical day for firefighters as winds gusted to 18 mph, with lower humidity and higher temperatures making containing the fire more difficult.  The fire was located about eight miles north of Interstate-10 and 1.5 miles east of U.S. Highway 441.  While no homes were damaged or destroyed, the fire burned to within a mile of the closest homes, which were located on Omar Terrace, about a mile east of U.S. 441 and the Deep Creek community.  At this point there are some 300 homes evacuated, affecting approximately 1,000 people.  48 structural fire units were situated around 350 homes in the immediate area of the fire to prevent loss. By Tuesday evening, the Florida portion of the Bugaboo Fire had increased to a total of 119,501 acres and was 50% contained.||After this point the fire remained within containment with burnout operations continuing around the perimeter through the remainder of the month. Rains from Tropical Storm Barry (6/1-6/2) aided with fire control efforts.

By late on the 8th Georgia State Road 94 and Florida State Road 2 (All one road with two designations) was closed from Moniac, Georgia to Fargo, Georgia due to the threat of the Bugaboo Scrub fire crossing SR 2 and poor visibility because of smoke in the area. By 4:00 p.m. EDT 500 people were evacuated from the vicinity of Taylor, Florida in northern Baker County and others were evacuated near Moniac, Georgia. The focus the fire fighting efforts from May 8th-10th was on structure protection in the Taylor and Baxter, Florida area.  Fire crews, along with private timber companies, were working along the flanks of the fire where conditions allowed, as well as, working to protect structures.  Fire crews and forest industry personnel were also strengthening lines on the southwest edge of the Georgia Bugaboo Scrub near Fargo. The Southern Area Blue Team assumed responsibility of the Florida Bugaboo Scrub Fire, South of State Road 2, on May 10th at 10:00 a.m. EDT. At this point the fire had burned nearly 26,746 acres.||From the 10th through the 13th the fire burned southwest toward Deep Creek and lake City in Columbia County causing evacuations in those areas and sporadic closings of Interstates 10 and 75 in the area. By the 13th the Florida Bugaboo Scrub Fire had burned 102,000 acres, Georgia Bugaboo had burned 131,718 acres and the totals for the two were 233, 718 acres and 570 persons were forced from their homes. Smoke from these large fires produced hazy conditions as far south as Miami over the weekend.  On May 14th, About 4:00 p.m. the fire jumped containment line in the Fairview Road area east of US-441, about five miles north of the Deep Creek community but was contained overnight.  At this time the fire was considered to be 50 percent contained.  Tuesday, May 15th became a critical day for firefighters as winds gusted to 18 mph, with lower humidity and higher temperatures making containing the fire more difficult.  The fire was located about eight miles north of Interstate-10 and 1.5 miles east of U.S. Highway 441.  While no homes were damaged or destroyed, the fire burned to within a mile of the closest homes, which were located on Omar Terrace, about a mile east of U.S. 441 and the Deep Creek community.  At this point there are some 300 homes evacuated, affecting approximately 1,000 people.  48 structural fire units were situated around 350 homes in the immediate area of the fire to prevent loss. By Tuesday evening, the Florida portion of the Bugaboo Fire had increased to a total of 119,501 acres and was 50% contained.||After this point the fire remained within containment with burnout operations continuing around the perimeter through the remainder of the month. Rains from Tropical Storm Barry (6/1-6/2) aided with fire control efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nonsupercell waterspout tracked from one mile north of the Marathon Airport toward the west-southwest, making landfall as a tornado shortly before 1300 EST on Palm Island, a private resort community.  The tornado moved back over the water as a waterspout and made a second, final landfall onto private property near the west end of Vaca Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large nonsupercell waterspout made landfall as a tornado on Palm Island, a private resort community.  Trees were damaged, along with air conditioning units torn off a commercial structure, and outdoor furniture tossed throughout the resort and into the water.  The tornado crossed back into the water, and made a second landfall just west of the Seven Mile Grille Restaurant on the Florida Bay side of Vaca Key.  There, only medium sized tree limbs were damaged.  Peak damage rating was confirmed as EF0.  The tornado dissipated as a weakening swirl across US Highway 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved south out of Miami-Dade County on the mainland, crossed the east end of Florida Bay, and produced sporadic wind damage throughout North Key Largo, Key Largo and Rock Harbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car dealership assistant manager reported strong winds blew open double glass doors and caused 30 feet of suspended ceiling to collapse.  A flag pole with two flags suspended was bent 30 degrees at a bank across the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring and early summer across parts of South Florida, combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006, produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into June across much of South Florida as  rainfall remained fairly sporadic, especially across interior and western sections of South Florida. D3 (extreme) drought conditions lasted around Lake Okeechobee until June 12th, with D2 (severe) drought conditions the remainder of the month. D2 drought conditions remained across Broward County until June 5th, eastern Palm Beach County until June 12th, and Collier County until June 19th when more consistent summer rains became more established across the area.||The level of Lake Okeechobee remained fairly steady, recovering only slightly from its low of 8.94 at the beginning of the month to 8.9 feet by June 29th. This slow change in the lake level resulted in a continuation of significant drought impacts for the general public, with water usage restrictions continuing across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in place for Broward County and the Lake Okeechobee area, while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across interior and western sections of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with gradually increasing  thunderstorm activity resulted in several new wildfires breaking out interior sections of South Florida. In addition, large wildfires which started in May at the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County and the Paterson Fire in Hendry County continued to burn into the first half of June. Over 65,000 acres were consumed between those two fires alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rain during the first half of September, along with long term rainfall deficits, resulted in the reinstatement of severe drought conditions for most of interior South Florida, including the Lake Okeechobee region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rain during the first half of September, along with long term rainfall deficits, resulted in the reinstatement of severe drought conditions for most of interior South Florida, including the Lake Okeechobee region. The level of Lake Okeechobee remained at record low levels of below 10 feet through the month, about 5 feet below normal levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced high winds, wind damage and large hail over parts of the Western Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced penny sized hail from Fort Walton Beach to Destin. Some vehicles had their windshields cracked by the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm initiated along the sea breeze front moved west across the Naples Airport, producing damage to aircraft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 62 mph reported by Naples ASOS (KAPF). An observer at Naples Airport reported that 3 small planes were flipped over and a hangar door was blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event began in the last week of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Noel passed well east of southeast Florida on November 1, with sustained winds of 25 to 30 mph reported across Palm Beach County. Highest gusts were in the 32 to 39 mph range across Palm Beach County.||The strongest winds and minor damage occurred on October 30 and 31 preceding Noel's closest passage to southeast Florida. Damage was minor and mainly confined to a few downed power lines. Around 5,000 customers lost power in the three-county area of Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade counties.||Rainfall amounts were rather light, ranging from a half-inch (0.5) to nearly two inches (1.94) 9 miles west of Sweetwater.||Lowest sea level pressures were in the 1003 to 1004 mb range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over most of interior and southwest Florida during November as the onset of the South Florida dry season is compounding long-term rainfall deficits across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The onset of the South Florida dry season in November has resulted in the continuation of the severe drought (D2) conditions across the region. November rainfall was below normal, and the level of Lake Okeechobee continued to set daily record lows of just over 10 feet above sea level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area about 80 miles west of the Southwest Florida coast provided for moisture and instability to spread across South Florida and resulted in thunderstorm development across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines reported down at Commercial Boulevard and NW 70th Avenue in Tamarac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the northeastern Gulf of Mexico along with moist southerly flow over South Florida produced afternoon thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maintenance building in Lauderhill was destroyed by a fire believed to have been caused by lightning. The 2,000 square foot building was a total loss.||Witnesses reported seeing lightning and looked outside to see the building on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong pressure gradient between a weak low pressure area over the western Bahamas and strong high pressure over the Northeast United States, along with deep moisture and instability east of an upper level low west of Naples, led to the formation of fast moving showers and thunderstorms which produced locally strong winds across Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. Severe thunderstorms were observed over Southeast Miami-Dade County, and strong non-thunderstorm winds caused beach erosion in Palm Beach County.||This episode continued into the first few days of October 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf with waves of 10-12 feet pounded the Palm Beach County shoreline from September 30 through October 2. Major beach erosion was observed on Jupiter Beach, Singer Island, Lantana and Delray Beach. About 12 feet of beach was lost at Lantana and Delray Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across portions of Collier County during November as the onset of the South Florida dry season is compounding long-term rainfall deficits across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry November led to the reinstatement of severe drought conditions (D2) to northern Collier County. Naples has only received less than 70 percent of its normal annual rainfall, with similar values inland. Water levels are low, caused by the below normal rainfall and the record low level of  Lake Okeechobee which was at just over 10 feet above sea level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate and persistent northeast flow between a surface high over the Eastern seaboard and a cold front across south Florida produced high surf and high astronomical tides along the Northeast Florida coast which caused beach erosion and coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10:54 am, the Ponce de Leon Inlet NOS tide gauge measured a maximum tide level of 5.04 ft. This was about 1.18 ft above predicted values. At 12:54 pm, the St. Johns County Life Guard Station reported strong rip currents and breaker heights of 4 ft. Minor coastal flooding at low beach entrances at Fort Mantanzas Inland and Porpoise Point was reported. There was a lot of beach erosion along county beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent east to northeast low level flow between Tropical Storm Noel south of the area and high pressure to the north produced high surf along the northeast Florida coast Nov. 1st-2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jacksonville Beach Life Guard Station reported breakers of 5 to 6 feet each morning with occasional breakers of 8 to 10 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent east to northeast low level flow between Tropical Storm Noel south of the area and high pressure to the north produced high surf along the northeast Florida coast Nov. 1st-2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported surf at Flagler Beach of 4 to 5 feet with occasional 6 to 8 feet breakers. The winds were blowing from the east northeast around 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent east to northeast low level flow between Tropical Storm Noel south of the area and high pressure to the north produced high surf along the northeast Florida coast Nov. 1st-2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported breaker heights of 4 to 6 feet at St. Augustine Beach with occasional 7 foot breakers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal band of convection moved across Northeast Florida during the early morning hours of December 16th before sunrise. There was about a week of near record high temperatures across the area in advance of this storm system, allowing for many days of pre-frontal instability to develop across the area.  There was also a strong 850 mb southwest jet around 55 knots which advected low level moisture and instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico overnight in advanced of the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down across the Meadowbrook Golf Course. The cost of tree removal was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low rapidly deepened over NC during the early morning hours of April 15, 2007, as the parent upper level trough phased with the surface feature. A squall line of severe storms preceding a cold front moved through northeast Florida during the early morning hours of April 15th and produced multiple supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were damaged about 3 miles west of Jacksonville Beach by thunderstorm wind gusts. One limb was about 12 feet long.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees and isolated power lines were blown down in northeast Baker county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and dry high pressure built across the region behind a strong cold front. Strong cold air advection through the day and radiational cooling under clear skies and calm winds that night allowed several locations across the northwest Florida peninsula to fall below freezing for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures were generally in the upper 20s across the county, with the Brooksville ASOS reporting 28 degrees and the FAWN station reaching 29. The average duration of below freezing temperatures was 4 hours, with up to 8 hours in isolated locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and dry high pressure built across the region behind a strong cold front. Strong cold air advection through the day and radiational cooling under clear skies and calm winds that night allowed several locations across the northwest Florida peninsula to fall below freezing for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures were generally around 30 across the county, with the RAWS station 12 miles southwest of Chiefland reporting 27 degrees and the FAWN station in Bronson reaching 32. The lowest temperature recorded was 26 degrees at the cooperative station 6 miles southeast of Chiefland. The average duration of below freezing temperatures was 5 hours, with up to 8 hours in isolated locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts along a rural road off of U.S. Highway 17, about 3 miles south of Bostwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed during the mid to late afternoon hours across the Lake Okeechobee region of South Florida, with 2 reports received of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail damaged crops in a field east of Belle Glade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing large hail affected Glades County for the second day in a row.

Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a late-season cold front and swept across large parts of South Florida during the evening hours of May 6th. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several motorists reported a tornado about 1 to 2 miles north of Alligator Alley - I-75 - between mile markers 48 and 51. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a wildfire near Felda on May 19th which lasted until June. On May 24th, the fire jumped the southern periphery and threatened 30 homes in Felda, although no homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a wildfire near Felda on May 19th which lasted until June. On May 24th, the fire jumped the southern periphery and threatened 30 homes in Felda, although no homes were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning on May 3rd sparked a wildfire in the Picayune Strand State Forest about 12 miles east of Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning on May 7th sparked a wildfire in the Picayune Strand State Forest about 12 miles east of Naples. On May 8th, the fire damaged 3 homes  in the Golden Gate Estates area and threatened 33 others. ||The fire consumed 18,029 acres and occurred during extreme drought conditions during the winter and spring of 2007.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring across South Florida combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006 produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal winter and spring rainfall along with a prolonged period of dry weather going back to early 2006 caused for severe drought conditions to spread southward across South Florida. As of May 29th, all of South Florida north of a West Palm Beach to Naples line was under D3 (extreme) drought status, with areas south of that line in a D2 (severe) drought status, with the exception of Miami-Dade County which was not considered to be in a drought.||Rainfall had begun to fall a little more regularly by the end of the month across eastern sections, but remained sporadic all month across interior and western areas.||The level of Lake Okeechobee continued to lower, and reached a all-time record low of 8.94 feet by May 31st. Significant impacts continued for the general public as water usage restrictions continued across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in effect while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across most of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with scattered thunderstorms early in the month resulted in several wildfires breaking out across South Florida as a result of lighning. One wildfire burned in the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County for over a month from a lightning strike on May 4th, with over 60,000 acres burned. Another wildfire in the Picayune Strand State Forest started from lightning on May 7th, burning over 18,000 acres. On May 8th, the fire damaged 3 homes in the Golden Gate Estates area and threatened 33 others. Yet another lightning-sparked wildfire in Hendry County started on May 19th, consuming over 4,000 acres and threatening homes in Felda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mayport National Oceans Service tide gauge measured 6.58 ft above Mean Lower Low Water (MLLW) which was about 0.8 to 1 ft above the predicted tide. Minor coastal flooding begins around 6.4 ft MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagler County Emergency Management Agency reported 7 to 11 foot breakers along Flagler Beach. They also reported a wind gust to 43 miles an hour from the northeast at 1010 am EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jacksonville Beach Red Cross Rescue Patrol reported minor coastal flooding of the street and parking lot. The flooding was due to wave runup from the high surf and the high tides due to a northeaster and was located on a beach access ramp between Red Cross building and a restaurant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface ridge over the Ohio Valley and mid-Atlantic and Tropical Storm Noel over the Caribbean produced a strong northeast flow regime over our local area. Strong and persistent onshore flow produced bands of heavy rainfall, high and rough surf as well as caused tidal flooding along our coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagler county EM reported the following rainfall totals since midnight of 2 to 5 inches across Flagler county. Some the highest amounts were 4.91 inches at the Flagler Emergency Operations Center in Bunnell and 5 inches in South Flagler Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface ridge over the Ohio Valley and mid-Atlantic and Tropical Storm Noel over the Caribbean produced a strong northeast flow regime over our local area. Strong and persistent onshore flow produced bands of heavy rainfall, high and rough surf as well as caused tidal flooding along our coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fernandina Beach NOS gauge reported 8.91 ft MLLW which was 1.41 ft above predicted tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stacked high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic region and low pressure over the Bahamas persisted for several days and brought deep tropical Atlantic moisture over our area under light steering flow. Periods of heavy rainfall during early October produced areal flooding in some areas across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 5 pm on 10/4, Emergency Management reported water covering roads about 4-5 inches deep about 6 miles northwest of Starke at the intersection of State Road 16 and Highway 225. Water was approaching some homes and power outage occurred. Around 510 pm, a one mile stretch of U.S. Highway 301 was closed in Starke due to flooding between State Road 16 and State Road 100. Water was up to 3 ft in some places. At 257 pm on 10/5, emergency management reported that State Road 100 was flooded but still passable near the Bradford-Clay county line. The county public works put caution signs near flooded areas. ||water is covering the road about 4 to 5 inches deep near  intersection of highway 16 and highway 225. water is near some houses. power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds of 15 to 20 mph caused rip currents to form along the Palm Beach County coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49-year-old man was rescued from a rip current by a town of Palm Beach police officer. The victim was swimming about 100 feet from shore near Chilean Avenue and South Ocean Boulevard. The police officer was flagged down by a bystander and was in the water rescuing the victim when Fire-Rescue arrived at the scene.||The swimmer and the police officer were both taken to the hospital and were reported in good condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over most of the interior and southwest sections of South Florida during December. Below normal rainfall during the month, in combination with record low levels of Lake Okeechobee, led to worsening drought conditions over Glades County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions (D2) continued over most of the interior and southwest sections of South Florida during December. Below normal rainfall going back to November has led to lowering of water levels, with some areas reporting levels at less than 10 percent of normal. Lake Okeechobee continued to set daily record low levels of just over 10 feet. Glades County was placed in an extreme drought condition (D3) during the second week of December due to very dry ground conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jacksonville Beach Life Guard Station reported breaker heights of 4-5 feet with some 6 foot breakers around 1029 am on 10/29. Around 11:54 am, the Mayport NOS tide gauge measured 6.82 ft MLLW which was 0.75 ft above predicted levels. At 1230 pm, ocean flooding produced 10-12 inches of water over campground sites at Talbot Island and a portion of Fort George was flooded. At 12:36 pm, the Fernandina Beach tide gauge measured 8.91 ft MLLW which was 1.41 ft above predicted levels. At 330 pm, the Jacksonville Beach Life Guards reported ocean water came through an adjacent beach access area to the Red Cross building and flooded portions of a parking lot. The guards also reported breakers of 5-8 ft with moderate to severe beach erosion. At 857 pm, Jacksonville Beach was closed where four boards designed to break away during high surf were knocked out, according to broadcast media. On 10/30, the Oak Landing tide gauge on the Intercoastal Waterway measured 4.4 ft above MLLW at 3:12 pm. Around 430 pm, water entered backyards near Oak Landing according to a NWS employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported water beginning to accumulate in normally flood prone areas of Riverside. Water was  reported to be ponding in the roadways near Saint Vincent���s  Hospital...on Stockton Street...the intersection of Kings street and Oak Street and on St Johns Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fernandina Beach National Oceans Service tide gauge measured 8.5 ft above Mean Lower Low Water (MLLW) which was 1.1 ft above predicted astronomical tide. Minor flooding begins at around 8.5 ft MLLW near Fernandina Beach and Amelia Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface ridge over the Ohio Valley and mid-Atlantic and Tropical Storm Noel over the Caribbean produced a strong northeast flow regime over our local area. Strong and persistent onshore flow produced bands of heavy rainfall, high and rough surf as well as caused tidal flooding along our coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Guana River State Park around 11:28 am on 10/29 the surf was around 8 ft. Around 11:30 am, rough waves and strong northeast winds sunk a boat at the harbor near the Bridge of Lions in downtown St. Augustine (no injuries). At noon, the back porch of a residence at 2561 South Ponte Vedra Blvd. was washed into the ocean. High surf and tides eroded about 12 feet of sand from this residence which was about 4 miles south of Guana River State Park. Stairs leading to the ocean nearby were washed away, and several backyards along the oceanfront were flooded. Minor street flooding was reported in Vilano Beach and many beach walkways were washed away. Around 6 pm, about 5 miles south of Guana River State Park, a home located at 2800 block of Ponte Vedra Beach Blvd. South was in danger of collapse due to coastal erosion. A 25-foot seawall had been built in front of the home a week prior, and the surf undermined the wall. About 3 to 7 feet of Ponte Vedra beach was eroded. Several roads had to be closed in St. Augustine due to flooding from the inlet which included Kings Street between Ponce De Leon Blvd. and Malaga Street. By 6 pm, high tides and 7-10 ft breakers produced severe beach erosion in the Summerhaven area north of Marineland. On 10/30 around 1:30 pm, the ocean reached the dunes about 3 miles south of Guana River State Park and was eroding the dune line. Several homes at 2855, 2859, and 2861 Ponte Vedra Blvd. South could have collapsed due to severe erosion. An empty lot at 2855 lost 10 feet of property between noon and 1:30 pm. Dirt was being dumped into the lot in an attempt to stabilize the properties on either side of it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were observed with a line of clouds and showers along the Broward County coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tower at Fort Lauderdale International Airport observed a funnel cloud with a line of showers just offshore the coast of Fort Lauderdale Beach. The funnel cloud only lasted a few minutes before dissipating, and never touched the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring and early summer across parts of South Florida, combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006, produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into June across much of South Florida as  rainfall remained fairly sporadic, especially across interior and western sections of South Florida. D3 (extreme) drought conditions lasted around Lake Okeechobee until June 12th, with D2 (severe) drought conditions the remainder of the month. D2 drought conditions remained across Broward County until June 5th, eastern Palm Beach County until June 12th, and Collier County until June 19th when more consistent summer rains became more established across the area.||The level of Lake Okeechobee remained fairly steady, recovering only slightly from its low of 8.94 at the beginning of the month to 8.9 feet by June 29th. This slow change in the lake level resulted in a continuation of significant drought impacts for the general public, with water usage restrictions continuing across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in place for Broward County and the Lake Okeechobee area, while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across interior and western sections of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with gradually increasing  thunderstorm activity resulted in several new wildfires breaking out interior sections of South Florida. In addition, large wildfires which started in May at the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County and the Paterson Fire in Hendry County continued to burn into the first half of June. Over 65,000 acres were consumed between those two fires alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a late-season cold front and swept across large parts of South Florida during the evening hours of May 6th. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a business building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning on May 19th caused a large wildfire near Felda. Several smaller lightning-sparked wildfires occurred elsewhere in Hendry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a wildfire near Felda on May 19th which lasted until June 6th, making it the longest lasting wildfire in Hendry County history. On May 24th, the fire jumped the southern periphery and threatened 30 homes in Felda, although no homes were damaged. The fire consumed a total of 4,450 acres.||Other smaller fires started by lightning burned across Hendry County in early June during a period of extreme drought conditions. These fires were contained by June 12th and did not damage any property.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and killed a 24-year old man who was laying tile on the roof of a home in western sections of Miramar. The force of the lightning knocked out a second worker, but he regained consciousness and did not require further medical attention.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the intersection of the Palmetto and Dolphin Expressways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated golf ball size hail near NW 20 Street and NW 22 Avenue in the Alapattah neighborhood of Miami.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail reported at Jackson Memorial Hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pilot along with several public reports of large hail in Downtown Miami. Several reports of cars with broken windows were received by law enforcement.

Three people drowned at southeast Florida beaches over the Memorial Day weekend as a result of rip currents. East winds of 15 to 20 mph persisted for several days and caused favorable conditions for numerous rip currents.||A total of 30 rescues were performed along Palm Beach County beaches alone, with many more likely to have occurred across all of southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56-year-old Slovakian man visiting on business drowned at the beach behind The Breakers in Palm Beach. Hotel lifeguards pulled the man out of the water but could not revive him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing east to southeast winds ahead of a low pressure area over the Gulf of Mexico which eventually became Tropical Storm Barry caused favorable conditions for rip currents along Atlantic beaches on May 31st. One person drowned in Delray Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 31-year-old Michigan man drowned while trying to help a friend who had been caught in a rip current. The drowning occurred at the public beach near Ocean and Atlantic Avenues in Delray Beach, after lifeguards had gone home for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed late in the afternoon along the east coast sea breeze, and moved east across northern Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported in Aventura near Turnberry Isle Country Club.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly below normal rain during October, along with long term rainfall deficits, resulted in the continuing of severe drought conditions for interior South Florida, including the Lake Okeechobee region. The level of Lake Okeechobee during October was between 10 feet and 10.40 feet, which set record low daily levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring across South Florida combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006 produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal winter and spring rainfall along with a prolonged period of dry weather going back to early 2006 caused for severe drought conditions to spread southward across South Florida. As of May 29th, all of South Florida north of a West Palm Beach to Naples line was under D3 (extreme) drought status, with areas south of that line in a D2 (severe) drought status, with the exception of Miami-Dade County which was not considered to be in a drought.||Rainfall had begun to fall a little more regularly by the end of the month across eastern sections, but remained sporadic all month across interior and western areas.||The level of Lake Okeechobee continued to lower, and reached a all-time record low of 8.94 feet by May 31st. Significant impacts continued for the general public as water usage restrictions continued across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in effect while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across most of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with scattered thunderstorms early in the month resulted in several wildfires breaking out across South Florida as a result of lighning. One wildfire burned in the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County for over a month from a lightning strike on May 4th, with over 60,000 acres burned. Another wildfire in the Picayune Strand State Forest started from lightning on May 7th, burning over 18,000 acres. On May 8th, the fire damaged 3 homes in the Golden Gate Estates area and threatened 33 others. Yet another lightning-sparked wildfire in Hendry County started on May 19th, consuming over 4,000 acres and threatening homes in Felda.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into June across much of South Florida as  rainfall remained fairly sporadic, especially across interior and western sections of South Florida. D3 (extreme) drought conditions lasted around Lake Okeechobee until June 12th, with D2 (severe) drought conditions the remainder of the month. D2 drought conditions remained across Broward County until June 5th, eastern Palm Beach County until June 12th, and Collier County until June 19th when more consistent summer rains became more established across the area.||The level of Lake Okeechobee remained fairly steady, recovering only slightly from its low of 8.94 at the beginning of the month to 8.9 feet by June 29th. This slow change in the lake level resulted in a continuation of significant drought impacts for the general public, with water usage restrictions continuing across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in place for Broward County and the Lake Okeechobee area, while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across interior and western sections of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with gradually increasing  thunderstorm activity resulted in several new wildfires breaking out interior sections of South Florida. In addition, large wildfires which started in May at the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County and the Paterson Fire in Hendry County continued to burn into the first half of June. Over 65,000 acres were consumed between those two fires alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a late-season cold front and swept across large parts of South Florida during the evening hours of May 6th. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Security fencing and weighted construction barricades blown down along the northeast perimeter of a condominium building under construction at the corner of NE 2 Ave and NE 2 Street in Downtown Miami.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning on May 4th sparked a wildfire which lasted for over a month at the Big Cypress National Preserve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from thunderstorms on May 4th caused several wildfires in the Big Cypress National Preserve in Interior Collier County. No homes were threatened, although I-75 had to be closed on several occasions due to dense smoke. A total of 63,599 acres were burned by the wildfires which lasted over a month. ||The fires occurred during a period of extreme drought which occurred in the spring of 2007.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Barry formed in the extreme southeast Gulf of Mexico on June 1st and moved northeast across Central Florida during the day on June 2nd. Effects on South Florida were relatively minor, except for a tornado which struck Cutler Bay in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 27-year-old man visiting from Connecticut drowned after being caught in a rip current at the beach near 80 South Ocean Boulevard in Delray Beach. Two friends who were also caught in the rip current but escaped, along with lifeguards tried to save the victim to no avail.||The rip currents occurred the day before Tropical Storm Barry moved across Central Florida. Winds were out of the east to southeast at around 20 mph, leading to the formation of rip currents. Seas were also rough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and dry high pressure built across the region behind a strong cold front. Strong cold air advection through the day and radiational cooling under clear skies and calm winds that night allowed several locations across the northwest Florida peninsula to fall below freezing for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures, generally around 30 degrees, occurred across mainly the western portion of the county, with the lowest temperature of 25 degrees recorded in East Nobleton. The average duration of below freezing temperatures was 4 hours, with up to 8 hours in isolated locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds over the Columbus Day weekend led to the formation of strong rip currents along the southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A middle-aged man was rescued from the surf after nearly drowning due to rip currents in Palm Beach at an unguarded beach south of Sunrise Avenue. The man was conscious and resting on shore when fire-rescue personnel arrived.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring and early summer across parts of South Florida, combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006, produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into June across much of South Florida as  rainfall remained fairly sporadic, especially across interior and western sections of South Florida. D3 (extreme) drought conditions lasted around Lake Okeechobee until June 12th, with D2 (severe) drought conditions the remainder of the month. D2 drought conditions remained across Broward County until June 5th, eastern Palm Beach County until June 12th, and Collier County until June 19th when more consistent summer rains became more established across the area.||The level of Lake Okeechobee remained fairly steady, recovering only slightly from its low of 8.94 at the beginning of the month to 8.9 feet by June 29th. This slow change in the lake level resulted in a continuation of significant drought impacts for the general public, with water usage restrictions continuing across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in place for Broward County and the Lake Okeechobee area, while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across interior and western sections of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with gradually increasing  thunderstorm activity resulted in several new wildfires breaking out interior sections of South Florida. In addition, large wildfires which started in May at the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County and the Paterson Fire in Hendry County continued to burn into the first half of June. Over 65,000 acres were consumed between those two fires alone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage photographs and eyewitness accounts from a police officer have determined that a tornado with 80 MPH winds caused damage near Dundee.  The track began near the Cypresswood Golf and Country Club then tracked east-northeast across Highway 27 into Dundee where it caused moderate damage to manufactured homes in Dell Lake Village before lifting. Severe of the manufactured homes were uninhabitable due to roof damage. Nine other manufactured homes received damage to awnings, car ports, and parts of the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Barry made landfall along the Florida west coast during the afternoon of June 2nd. The storm then advanced north-northeast along the Atlantic coast through June 3rd. The main impacts of the storm on our area were heavy rains and gusty winds, which downed trees. Storm total rainfall amounts neared 6 inches across portions of the forecast area. Wind advisories were issued for coastal zones and lake wind advisories for the remainder of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain began affecting Flagler county early in the morning. Around 755 am local time, emergency management reported 3 inches of rain had fallen at Flagler Beach, 6-8 ft breakers and that Flagler Beach Pier had to be closed. Wind gusts were around 38 mph about one block inland.  Around 822 am, a tree had been blown down on Underwood Trail about 2 miles south-southwest of Flagler Beach by a strong wind gust in a squall. By noon, 5 inches of rain was measured about two miles south of Flagler Beach. By 529 pm local time, spotters in Palm Coast had measured around 4 inches of rainfall in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An un-seasonal amplified upper level trough and attendant surface front moved into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong differential positive vorticity advection, high surface based instability, large CAPE in the hail growth zone and low level forcing spawned widespread severe storms. Aloft cool 500 mb around -10 degrees Celsius were noted and mid level dry air moved over the area, and both factors increased downdraft strength in storms.

Around 406 pm local time, winds produced damage in Surfside Estates, a mobile home park located at 2847 North Ocean Shore Blvd. Two mobile homes were destroyed and two had minor damage. A billboard was blown down in Painters Hill. Around 416 pm local time, spotters measured wind gusts to 58 mph in Flagler Beach and one measured a gust to 77 mph. Around 418 pm local time, a 1,000 pound sign was blown off a restaurant roof in Flagler Beach. Around the same time, an ice cream parlor sign was snapped off and landed on the owner's vehicle which totaled the car. Around 420 pm local time, power lines were down across the central part of the county. A canopy on the Flagler Beach pier was blown off and caused one injury.

A dry spring across South Florida combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006 produced drought conditions across a large part of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal winter and spring rainfall along with a prolonged period of dry weather going back to early 2006 caused for severe drought conditions to spread southward across South Florida. As of April 24th, all of Glades and Hendry counties, as well as northwestern Palm Beach County was under D3 (extreme) drought status. Most of the remainder of Palm Beach County as well as northern sections of Collier and northwestern sections of Broward counties were under D2 (severe) drought status. ||By the end of April, Naples had received only 2.93 inches of rain for the year, 36 percent of normal. Fort Lauderdale, Clewiston and Belle Glade stood around 20 inches below normal precipitation since January 2006, about 65 percent of normal.||The level of Lake Okeechobee lowered further to 9.8 feet by April 27th, about 4.5 feet below average and the lowest level since May 2001. Impacts spread from strictly agricultural interest to the general public as water usage restrictions were placed across South Florida. The Lake Okeechobee region was placed under Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction).

Strong rip currents and rough surf impacted the Brevard County beaches for over a week. A man who was in waist deep water at Cocoa Beach Pier was pulled out to sea by the strong rip currents and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 year old Kissimmee resident who was in waist deep water near the Cocoa Beach Pier was pulled out to sea by strong rip currents. Bystanders pulled him out of the water, but he was in cardiac arrest and paramedics were unable to revive him. Other swimmers in the area were also caught in rip currents, but either managed to swim to safety or were assisted to shore by others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall since early 2006 produced drought conditions across the Lake Okeechobee region of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal rainfall since early 2006 produced severe drought conditions across the Lake Okeechobee region of South Florida. Large portions of Glades, Hendry and northern Palm Beach counties were under D2 (severe) drought status. The level of Lake Okeechobee lowered to 10.55 feet, about 3 feet below normal and the lowest  level since May 2001. Impacts were primarily to agriculture, with 90 percent of the area's topsoil moisture rated very short to short, and 80 percent of pastures rated very poor to poor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon thunderstorms lead to the injury of a firefighter responding to a fire originally ignited by lightning.

Lightning ignited a fire between Sand Road and 40th Street on the 27th that was contained but flared up into a 2,000 acre fire on the 28th.  The 5 mile long and 1 mile wide fire was the largest in Cape Coral's history and led to the evacuation of residents and the closure of a 6 miles stretch of Burnt Store Road. About 22 fire departments and 123 firefighters battled the blaze which came within 60 feet of eight homes in Sanctuary Estates.  Much of the land that burned was in a protected area.   No injuries or damage to structures were reported.
The interactions between the west coast sea breeze front and outflow boundaries from thunderstorms combined to create scattered thunderstorms in the interior counties of Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gabrielle, located off the Carolina Coast, produced rough surf along the Brevard County beaches for several days. A man surfing at Cocoa Beach fell off his board and drowned in the rough surf. Scores of others were rescued from the dangerous conditions throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56 year old local man surfing in rough surf off Robert P. Murkshe Memorial Park at 16th Street in Cocoa Beach fell off his board. When he continued to float in the water, a teenager swam out and pulled the unconscious man to shore where other bystanders performed CPR but were unable to resuscitate him. An autopsy report confirmed he died of drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of high surf, rip currents affected the Volusia County beaches for several days. A man swimming in the ocean got caught in a rip current. The man was rescued and taken to a local hospital where he remained in critical condition for a week, but then died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men and a woman were swimming off an unguarded part of Daytona Beach Shores on November 6 when they got caught in a rip current. As the rip current pulled them into deeper water, several people tried to assist them. Although all were returned to shore, one of them, a 65 year old male tourist from Colorado did not have a heartbeat or pulse. After being transported to a local hospital and being placed on life support, he regained a pulse, but then died on November 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term rainfall deficits continue to result in drought conditions over portions of interior and southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted over interior and southwest sections of South Florida as long term rainfall deficits and relative lack of summer rainfall continue. The level of Lake Okeechobee continued to rise only modestly, with lake levels remaining well below 10 feet, 5 feet below normal. Increasing rains by the second week of August were sufficient enough to downgrade the drought status from severe to moderate.

Water intrusion was reported at an apartment complex at 1040 Michigan Avenue in South Beach, and water was encroaching on homes along and near Alton Road between 44th and 48th Streets. ||Law enforcement also reported that South Beach south of 17th Street was under water with numerous streets flooded. Flooding was aggravated by high tide which occurred at 1142 AM EDT. Flooding also occurred along|Venetian Causeway which closed the road temporarily.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface ridge over the Ohio Valley and mid-Atlantic and Tropical Storm Noel over the Caribbean produced a strong northeast flow regime over our local area. Strong and persistent onshore flow produced bands of heavy rainfall, high and rough surf as well as caused tidal flooding along our coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagler Beach Fire Department reported breakers of 7-8 feet with some occasionally higher breakers around 10:29 am on 10/29. By 11:30 am, emergency management reported that the Flagler Beach pier was closed around 10 am due to high surf. Minor beach erosion began and the surf was around 6-7 feet. On 10/30, Flagler Beach reported surf of 6-7 feet. Later that day around 2:30 pm, water was over the seawall in Palm Coast. Around 3:05 pm, water was over the seawall by 6 inches in Palm Coast. Tides were running about 1 to 2 feet above predicted values.  Around 2:28 pm on 10/29, moderate beach erosion was reported along the Flagler county coast. There were 6 dues closed on the north side and four on the side side of the county coast. Around 3 pm, tidal flooding at the county owned Princess Estates Preserve was reported. The campground along Pelicier Creek has tidal flooding in 3 campsites with water up to 2 feet deep. Tidal flooding was also observed on the Stiles Creek Causeway into the campground where standing water caused hazardous driving conditions which prompted the county to close campground temporarily. Around 330-345 pm on 10/30, high tides rose the water 1 inch from the top of the seawall along the inland Palm Coast salt water canal. Princess Estates Preserve Park was closed due to water from Stiles Creek which rose 3 inches from the main road going into the park. A few trees fell down across the county due to saturated grounds. One tree along Ballard Lane in Palm Coast fell on a parked vehicle (no injuries).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused sporadic wind damage across much of Franklin County including Apalachicola during the pre-dawn hours of April 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds downed numerous trees throughout the county.  There was damage to several boats and businesses.  The metal roof was peeled off a seafood house and piled along U.S. Highway 98.  The winds capsized a shrimp boat at the Apalachicola City Dock.  Windows were blown out of several homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate and persistent northeast flow between a surface high over the Eastern seaboard and a cold front across south Florida produced high surf and high astronomical tides along the Northeast Florida coast which caused beach erosion and coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 12:42 pm, The Jacksonville Beach Life Guard Station reported 3-5 ft breakers and water over parking spaces at the end of the beach access parking lot. Some beach erosion also occurred. The tide was up to the dunes. At this time, the Mayport NOS tide gauge measured 6.36 ft MLLW which was about 0.45 ft above predicted values. Minor flooding usually begins around 6.4 ft. Earlier, at 10:54 am, The NOS tide gauge on the Main Street Bridge measured a river level of 2.58 ft. This was about 1.07 ft above predicted levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large high pressure center over the mid-Atlantic states caused cold air damming along the eastern seaboard and Peidmont with a strong coastal trough developing along the northeast Florida coast. The tightened low level pressure gradient when combined with strong upper level diffluence led to minor coastal flooding and areal rainfall flooding in Duval, St Johns, and Flagler Counties of north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 5.13 inches of rainfall since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal band of convection moved across Northeast Florida during the early morning hours of December 16th before sunrise. There was about a week of near record high temperatures across the area in advance of this storm system, allowing for many days of pre-frontal instability to develop across the area.  There was also a strong 850 mb southwest jet around 55 knots which advected low level moisture and instability inland from the Gulf of Mexico overnight in advanced of the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF1 path length was estimated at 3/4 mile and averaged about 200 yards wide. Maximum winds were estimated around 90-95 mph based on the damage survey. Numerous pine trees were snapped and a few were uprooted. A portion of a masonry brick wall on a small farm outbuilding was knocked over and the metal roof was ripped off and strewn across a field. A farm truck as flipped on its side near the outbuilding. Another house had a damaged carport, minor wall damage, a toppled TV antenna mast and many of the roof shingles were stripped. This location was along Northwest 93rd Avenue, northwest of Gainesville. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front interacted with sea breezes and afternoon instability to produce scattered severe storms across northeast Florida during the afternoon of May 6, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and power lines were blown down from strong thunderstorm wind gusts from Espanola to Palm Coast. Trees were blown down in Espanola.  Power lines were blown down in Palm Coast including one along Burning Sand Road.  Another power line was blown down along Patrick Drive and Milwaukee  in Palm Coast and it sparked a fire.  Another tree was blown down along Squire Court in Palm Coast. We received a report of estimated thunderstorms gusts to 65 mph at 1328hrs in Palm Coast. We received another report of estimated 60 mph winds at the Bunnell, Flagler County Airport.

One tree was reported down on a power line in Palm Coast at 10:05 EST and a second tree was blown down on John Anderson Highway in Flagler Beach at 10:22 EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 10 inches of rain was measured at the Pennekamp State Park rangers quarters ending 09:00 EST on October 1, 2007. Street flooding was observed in the vicinity of the cooperative station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms over the Upper Keys produced heavy rainfall over and a damaging lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the cupola of an unoccupied large oceanfront home in Plantation Key. The strike caused a small fire and damaged plumbing within the roof structure of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued into April with several major wildfires and numerous minor events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45 acre wildfire near Middleburg spread due to low relative humidity and threatened more than 50 homes before the fire was brought under control around 7 p.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued into March with wildfires breaking out in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Described as the Railroad Bed Fire with 130 acres burned west of Interstate 95 and south of County Road 108.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front interacted with sea breezes and afternoon instability to produce scattered severe storms across northeast Florida during the afternoon of May 6, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a report of 1.00��� of rain from a skywarn spotter from the Pine Lakes Area of Palm Coast. received a report of 1.20��� of rain from a Skywarn Spotter at Indian Trails in  Palm Coast.  Received a report of 1.20��� of rain at Pine Lakes from a Skywarn spotter. Received a report of 3.75��� of rain from a Skywarn spotter in Grand Haven in Palm Coast. Received a report of 1.20��� of rain in West Central Palm Coast in the City of Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of strong thunderstorms produced widespread heavy rain totals from Key West through Big Pine Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bucket survey conducted by NWS personnel indicated a spot rainfall total of approximately 11.50 inches from two consecutive convective rainfall events on Little Torch Key.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the west coast sea breeze front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven apartments were damaged by a fire started by a lightning strike on the 3700 block of Winkler Avenue. Firefighters arrived on the scene three minutes after the first call and helped evacuate residents from the building. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout and at least two tornadoes formed along the west Florida coast sea breeze front in the Fort Myers area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Down burst winds wrapping around a tornado three blocks to the east caused minor damage to several homes in the area around the intersection of Beach Parkway West and Agualinda Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced several reports of wind damage over portions of the Florida Big Bend during the late afternoon and early evening of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wakulla County EMA reported a few downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed in north Santa Rosa county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud along State Highway 87 near the Florida-Alabama state line.  The funnel never touched down and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout and at least two tornadoes formed along the west Florida coast sea breeze front in the Fort Myers area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued to develop northward along the sea breeze front and produced a tornado that traveled due north from near the intersection of Rose Garden and and El Dorado Parkway West to near Skyline Boulevard and Veterans memorial Parkway. The damage path was less than the size of a single family home for most of the track and the average wind speed based on the damage was 65-75 MPH.  The EF1 damage was of a home nearly cut in half by the tornado with wind speeds estimated at 105 MPH.  The lower than expected wind estimate was a result of older building codes and the roof blowout occurred as a result of a large picture window being blown in.  About 138 homes were damaged.  Six homes received moderate damage and one home was destroyed. The tornado snapped several power poles and damaged numerous fences, sheds and trees.  Several vehicles and boats were flipped over. There was one injury as a gentleman looked outside to see the tornado and was struck by debris. Thirty people were displaced from their homes. Damage estimates were based on a survey completed by Cape Coral Emergency Management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure formed in the eastern Gulf of Mexico in association with an upper level low. Shower and thunderstorm activity with this non-tropical system was limited but the bands of precipitation generally rotated north throughout the day in the Tampa Bay area and the motion curved to the northwest in the Cedar Key and The Villages area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust downed two trees and killed a farm animal.  The home weather station recorded 52 MPH winds before being knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure formed in the eastern Gulf of Mexico in association with an upper level low. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust was measured by a weather station of a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home near Pace.  The strike started a fire and the home was destroyed.
A funnel cloud was observed by Law Enforcement for several minutes near Lillian Highway.  The funnel dissipated and never touched the ground.

A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the eastern Florida Panhandle and western Florida Big Bend during the afternoon of February 13, and produced damaging winds, large hail and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Liberty County EMA reported some downed trees.  A roof was blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the eastern Florida Panhandle and western Florida Big Bend during the afternoon of February 13, and produced damaging winds, large hail and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado briefly touched down and destroyed a large newly constructed shed.  A roof was blown off a house.  Numerous pine trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced several reports of wind damage over portions of the Florida Big Bend during the late afternoon and early evening of February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wakulla County EMA reported several downed trees throughout the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough over the eastern United States focused moisture and dynamics over our area. Upper level forcing from the trough along with afternoon instability and the sea breezes spawned widespread severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a pine tree in a courtyard near Greenbrier Apartments off of Justina Road in Arlington. The lightning charge then dispersed through an adjacent puddle and killed one man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was stuck in car due to flooding on Algonquin Ave and flooding reported was on Toree Grande Ave. and Casa Grande Ave. as well as in the Beauclerc area. Flooding was also reported on San Jose Boulevard and near Julington Creek Plantation. Numerous secondary streets were reported as flooded on the Southside. Commonwealth Road was reported as being flooded at 07:00 north of Interstate 295 with vehicles stranded in the roadway. In the San Marco Area flooding was observed by NWS Storm Survey Crews from Childrens Way to Hendricks Avenue up to Nemours Hosptial just south of Interstate 95. The water was nearly up to residences on Childrens Way. Flooding was also observed in the Plantation Oaks South subdivision in Mandarin on Carters Grove Lane at 1700 Plantation Oak Drive with the road flooded and impassable. The Storm Survey also noted several inches of water in the home and garage of a residence located at 11665 Gywnford Lane. This is near the intersection of Loretto Road. At this location water was accumulated in the backyard to a depth of one foot. At McCoys Creek four feet of flooding was noted in a roofing business at Kings Street and McCoys Creek Blvd. Four homes were flooded to a depth of 1.5 to 2 feet at 4130 Leonard Circle West. Additionally water from Moncrief Creek crossed 33rd Street West and pushed a Toyota Corolla against the bridge resulting in a swift water rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of a cold front over the eastern Gulf of Mexico spawned a waterspout which moved onshore along the northern Collier County coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore along Pelican Bay's North Beach, causing damage to several structures at the Sandpiper Restaurant. The awning covering the restaurant's outdoor dining area was partially ripped off and part of its metal frame was damaged. Lounge chairs were thrown through several of the restaurant's windows, with chairs and beach umbrellas observed as far inland as Pelican Bay Boulevard, about a half-mile inland. ||Five vertical propane heaters were destroyed, and a 5-foot by 8-foot section of the roof was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with the remnants of Tropical Depression Olga to produce hazardous weather in west central Florida. The tornado formed from a long-lived supercell thunderstorm that moved onshore from the Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A housing annex worth $750,000 at the county jail was destroyed. The 125 female inmates living in the annex were evacuated before it was destroyed, resulting in zero injuries. There was also roof damage to a fire station, four vehicles were flipped and there were numerous trees and power poles downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced straight-line wind damage to fences, shingles and tree limbs near Navarre. The storm began near Holley By The Sea where a home had major roof and garage damage.  The storm moved northeast and produced minor roof and tree damage before dissipating  near the Holley NOLF landing field.

Thunderstorm wind damaged occurred around the Milton area.  The damage was caused by a series of mesocyclone-induced microbursts.  The thunderstorm first caused minor tree and outbuilding damage just southwest of Milton around 1055 PM.  The thunderstorm moved northeast causing more damage along Highway 90 near the intersection of Highway 87 in Milton.  A lot of the damage in this area was caused by rocks being picked up by the wind and then thrown into the air and breaking several windows.  Three vehicles at a car dealership had windows knocked out.  An insurance building also had some windows blown out and the wind caused ceiling damage inside the building.  The storm moved across the Milton area around 1115 PM.  The thunderstorm continued moving northeast along Highway 191 and damaged two manufactured homes just northeast of the Roeville community.  The wind struck the homes broadside and moved both of them off their blocks.  Near this same area, a brick home had some bricks blown off the side of the house.  This was likely caused by wind blowing around the home, creating turbulence that shook the bricks loose.  The storm stopped producing damage around 1140 PM.  No injuries occurred.  Several people reported seeing a funnel cloud, but the funnel never touched down.

A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event began in the last week of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Noel passed well east of southeast Florida on November 1, with sustained winds of 25 to 30 mph reported across southeast Florida. Highest sustained winds were 34 mph at Miami Beach. Highest gusts were in the 32 to 39 mph range across the area, with a peak wind gust of 43 mph at Miami Beach.||The strongest winds and minor damage occurred on October 30 and 31 preceding Noel's closest passage to southeast Florida. Damage was minor and mainly confined to a few downed power lines. Around 5,000 customers lost power in the three-county area of Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade counties.||Rainfall amounts were rather light, ranging from a half-inch (0.5) to nearly two inches (1.94) 9 miles west of Sweetwater.||Lowest sea level pressures were in the 1003 to 1004 mb range.
