EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and dry high pressure built into the area behind a cold front, bringing freezing temperatures to the northern counties each morning. Winds of around 10 mph the morning of Dec. 17th kept wind chill values in the upper 20s to lower 30s from Desoto and Highlands counties northward, but helped to prevent a widespread freeze or frost from developing across all but the normally colder locations across the northern area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were in place across the county for a few hours with a low of 31 at the Brooksville ASOS (KBKV) and the COOP site 4 miles north of Brooksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event began in the last week of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf associated with Tropical Storm Noel continued to batter the Palm Beach County coast. Hardest hit spots were beaches in Jupiter, Singer Island and South Palm Beach/Lantana, where severe to locally extreme beach erosion occurred. A steel sea wall protecting the Condado condominium complex in Singer Island collapsed, causing cracks to form in the outer walls of the building. In some areas, the dune line was completely eroded, with oceanfront buildings sitting on top of 15 foot cliffs straight down to the water. A sea wall at the Imperial House condominiums in South Palm Beach collapsed from the pounding surf, and the east portion of the building was evacuated. South of Lantana to Boca Raton, erosion was reported as moderate to severe.||Total damage is estimated at $4 million.||On November 3, a dive boat capsized in the choppy surf at Boynton Beach Inlet near Manalapan, tossing all 5 occupants into the water. All five people were taken to the hospital but their injuries were not believed to be life-threatening.||Maximum storm tide occurred just before midnight on November 1st, with the Virginia Key NOS tide gauge reading 3.5 feet, and a maximum surge height of 1.08 feet. No tide measurements are available from Palm Beach County, but storm tide is estimated to have been as high as 2-3 feet over northern Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event lasted into the first few days of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf began to impact the Miami-Dade County coast on October 29th, with water levels rising sufficiently enough to cause minor to locally moderate beach erosion beginning on October 30th, and continuing through October 31st and into the first few days of November. Areas most affected by the beach erosion were sections of Miami Beach near 29th and 44th streets. ||Maximum storm tide observed at Virginia Key NOS tide gauge was 3.5 feet just before midnight on November 1, and the maximum surge height at that time was 1.08 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event began in the last week of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf continued to impact the Broward County as Tropical Storm Noel moved east and northeast of the region, with water levels rising sufficiently enough to cause moderate to isolated pockets of severe beach erosion and coastal flooding. Areas most affected by the beach erosion were Fort Lauderdale Beach and Hollywood Beach. A section of Highway A1A from Sunrise Boulevard to NE 21 Street in Fort Lauderdale Beach was closed on November 3 and 4 as a result of water washing over the roadway. ||Maximum storm tide observed at Virginia Key NOS tide gauge was 3.5 feet just before midnight on November 1, and the maximum surge height at that time was 1.08 feet. No tide measurements are available from Broward County, but storm tide is estimated to have been as high as 1-2 feet over portions of Broward County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49-year-old man was struck and killed by lightning while working on a vegetable farm in rural southern Hendry County.

The public reported that an underground garage of a hotel in Sunny Isles Beach was flooded with about 4 feet of water due to the high surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low over the Suwannee River Valley in concert with afternoon sea breezes produced scattered severe storms across north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail near Interlachen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure center generated severe weather across north Florida on the night of the 1st-2nd March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three single wide mobile homes with significant damage to siding, minor roof damage and damage to steps, fences, etc.  Used EF Scale - Damage Indicator (DI) MHSW with a Degree of Damage (DOD) #2 to the structures. Several Small farm buildings and sheds were moved or blown over with large tractors and equipment inside.  For these I used a DI of SBO and DOD #7. By crossing the two determined an EF-0 Scale rating with 80 mph wind speeds most likely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major squall line with multiple supercell thunderstorms moved across north and central Florida. While no tornado touchdowns occurred in the Jacksonville area of responsibility several significant touchdowns occurred just south of Marion and Flagler Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell on mobile home trapping person inside. Trees blown down and did damage to two homes resulting in two injuries in one of the homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents affected Southeast Florida beaches over New Year's Weekend 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 70 year old tourist from Pennsylvania drowned while fighting rip currents at an unguarded beach behind the Pink Palms Condominiums at the 2100 block of North Ocean Drive in Fort Lauderdale Beach.  |This was the second tourist to drown as a result of rip currents during the New Year's Weekend of 2007. Unseasonably warm weather along with strong east winds attracted hundreds of beach goers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major squall line with multiple supercell thunderstorms moved across north and central Florida. While no tornado touchdowns occurred in the Jacksonville area of responsibility several significant touchdowns occurred just south of Marion and Flagler Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread power outages in Bunnell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major squall line with multiple supercell thunderstorms moved across north and central Florida. While no tornado touchdowns occurred in the Jacksonville area of responsibility several significant touchdowns occurred just south of Marion and Flagler Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurring at report time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of arctic cold fronts moved through South Florida on President's Day Weekend and produced freezing temperatures across much of South Florida on the mornings of February 17th and 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures affected interior sections of South Florida behind an arctic cold front which moved across the area the previous day. Low temperatures ranged from 27 degrees at Palmdale to 31 degrees at Belle Glade, South Bay and Ortona, to 32 degrees at LaBelle, Clewiston,  Immokalee and Royal Palm Beach West. Unofficial observations indicated that temperatures reached the mid and upper 20s in fields near Belle Glade and elsewhere in western Palm Beach County.|Damage to crops was significant in western Palm Beach County, with an estimated $20-50 million in losses to the green bean and sweet corn crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing shot of arctic air moved into South Florida late on February 18th, with freezing temperatures recorded once again overnight into the early morning hours on February 19th. Temperatures ranged from 26 degrees at Palmdale to 31 degrees at Immokalee, Ortona, South Bay and Royal Palm Ranger Station to 32 degrees at Homestead, Moore Haven, Monroe Station, LaBelle and Chekika State Park. |Some relatively minor crop damage to green beans and squash was noted in far South Miami-Dade County, but damage estimates were less than 30 percent of crop. Overall crop damage was not as extensive as with the freeze 2 days prior in Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of arctic cold fronts moved through South Florida on President's Day Weekend and produced freezing temperatures across much of South Florida on the mornings of February 17th and 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing shot of arctic air moved into South Florida late on February 18th, with freezing temperatures recorded once again overnight into the early morning hours on February 19th. Temperatures ranged from 26 degrees at Palmdale to 31 degrees at Immokalee, Ortona, South Bay and Royal Palm Ranger Station to 32 degrees at Homestead, Moore Haven, Monroe Station, LaBelle and Chekika State Park. |Some relatively minor crop damage to green beans and squash was noted in far South Miami-Dade County, but damage estimates were less than 30 percent of crop. Overall crop damage was not as extensive as with the freeze 2 days prior in Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of arctic cold fronts moved through South Florida on President's Day Weekend and produced freezing temperatures across much of South Florida on the mornings of February 17th and 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures affected interior sections of South Florida behind an arctic cold front which moved across the area the previous day. Low temperatures ranged from 27 degrees at Palmdale to 31 degrees at Belle Glade, South Bay and Ortona, to 32 degrees at LaBelle, Clewiston,  Immokalee and Royal Palm Beach West. Unofficial observations indicated that temperatures reached the mid and upper 20s in fields near Belle Glade and elsewhere in western Palm Beach County.|Damage to crops was significant in western Palm Beach County, with an estimated $20-50 million in losses to the green bean and sweet corn crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gusty cold front passed through the area on Sunday February 18th. Cold high pressure quickly settled over the area Sunday night. Calm winds along the Suncoast allowed temperatures to reach freezing for three or more hours. A light northeast winds kept most areas along and south of Interstate 4 above freezing. However, inland low spots did record temperatures briefly in the upper 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing area of low pressure over the western Gulf of Mexico on Thursday, February 1 intensified and moved off the mid Atlantic coast Friday.  Numerous thunderstorms developed out ahead of and along the associated cold front that pushed into southern Florida late Friday. The first tornado of the year to use the Enhanced Fujita Scale occurred just after 3 AM Friday in The Villages in northeast Sumter County.  Several violent tornadoes were reported across central Florida early Friday morning. This violent tornado outbreak was the largest in Florida since the  Kissimmee tornadoes of February 22-23, 1998. |In addition to the tornadoes, gusty southwest winds ahead of the cold front combined with high tide to cause areas of minor coastal flooding along west and southwest facing shorelines north of Tampa Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported by the pubulic along Highway 19 in Crystal River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down near the southeast corner of The Villages south of Lake Miona and moved rapidly to the east northeast at 55 MPH into Lake County. A National Weather Service storm survey estimated maximum winds of 140 MPH. No fatalities were recorded in Sumter County and 15 injuries is  a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of powerful tornadoes swept across Sumter, Lake and Volusia counties during the early morning hours of February 2, 2007. A discontinuous swath of tornado damage was observed from near Wildwood in Sumter County to the town of Lady Lake (Lake County) to New Smyrna Beach (Volusia County). This was a distance of over 70 miles. A total of 21 fatalities occurred within Lake County. Central Florida was located in the warm sector ahead of an advancing cold front. Large scale lift was supported by a very strong jet aloft, with strong vertical shear. Instability increased overnight with temperatures and dew points increasing through the pre-dawn hours. A single long-tracked supercell thunderstorm traveled from Sumter County to the coastal waters of Volusia County during the early morning hours of February 2, 2007. Three tornadoes touched down along this track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The the tornado that moved out of Sumter County, crossed into Lake County just west of Lady Lake. The one quarter mile wide, F3 tornado moved across the Lady Lake area killing 8 people and injuring 10. The tornado damaged 180 residences and destroyed 101. All of the fatalities were in mobile homes. Moving east northeast at 50 MPH, the tornado lifted near Emrald Marsh Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesocyclone that produced the Lady Lake tornado reintensified and produced a second F3 tornado in a rural area west southwest of Paisley. The tornado move east northeast at over 50 MPH toward the Lake Mack area just west of the St. Johns river. The tornado produced a quarter mile wide track across the Lake Mack area destroying 86 residences and damaging 144.  Many mobile homes were completely destroyed. Thirteen people died in mobile homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same mesocyclone that produced the previous tornadoes in Lake and Volusia County now moved over New Smyrna Beach. The F1 tornado first touched down east of Interstate 95 along Turnbull Bay Road and moved rapidly east where it lifted at the Intracoastal Waterway just north of New Smyrna Beach Airport. The tornado damaged roofs, car ports, garage doors and pool screens in the area. There were no injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that killed 13 people in the Lake Mack area of Lake County  moved east across the St. Johns River, crossed into Volusia county at Lake Beresford and moved across Deland as an F3 tornado. It was still moving east northeast at 50 MPH as it damaged 277 and destroyed 106 residences in the Deland area. The tornado finally lifted about eight miles east of Deland along Highway 44. There were no fatalities in Volusia County but 42 people were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along a developing warm front in the Florida Straits. Several thunderstorm cells tracked northeast across Duck Key and Long Key, producing very heavy rainfall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Autamated Cooperative Weather Observing Station at Long Key State Park recorded 5.38 inches of rain at 0940 AM EST in two hours, with final storm total rainfall of 9.01 inches at 1317 PM EST.  State Park Rangers reported 6 campsites were washed out with park road flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold snap resulted in record cold temperatures across the eastern half of the United States with record cold temperatures for the month of April and record late freezes reported locally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record low temperature was set for both the date and month of April along with the latest freeze ever in Jacksonville. The low temperature fell to 31 degrees at 6:10 a.m. EDT breaking the old record of 37 degrees set in 1971. This also breaks the record low temperature for the month of April with the previous record low temperature for April being 34 in 1987. This was also the latest freeze in Jacksonville records with previous date of the last freeze being March 31st 1964 when the low temperature dropped to 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the southeast region brought a strong cold pool over the area. High low level instability and energy rotating around the upper low as well as low level boundaries including old outflows and sea breezes spawned widespread severe weather across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touchdown was reported by the public near the intersection of Interstate 95 and James T. Butler Blvd. No damage nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late evening sea breeze induced storm produced a weak tornado that touchdown down in Marion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado flipped over a car and damaged a total of six homes Silver Spring Shores. The Orlando Sentinel newspaper reported damage in Leeward Air Ranch, off of Baseline Road where six homes were damaged. Roofs were damaged, and hangars that housed private airplanes were damaged. At 8940 Southeast 79th Avenue, a home suffered damage which included a large hole in the roof and a large tree was blown down. At 8798 Southeast 89th Place, a roof was damaged on an abandoned home. The tornado began around Southeast 70th Terrace and traveled around 500 yards to Southeast 72nd Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fernandina Beach, Florida...a Stone Mountain, Georgia man visiting the area drowned when attempting to rescue his seven year old son from high surf and rip currents. A northeast flow of 20 to 30 mph had generated high surf and his son was swept offshore. The gentleman and two others attempted to rescue him, but were swept offshore themselves. Ocean rescue saved the son and the other two rescuers; however, the father drowned. The mother and son were treated and released at a local hospital.

He was swept out around 3 p.m. and was recovered at 4 p.m. approximately 200 yards north of where he disappeared. He was taken to the hospital where he died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off Northeast Florida which eventually became Subtropical Storm Andrea produced very large swells which pounded Southeast Florida beaches on May 7th and 8th. The greatest extent and magnitude of damage occurred on the 8th where severe beach erosion and flooding was observed along the Palm Beach County coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large swells of up to 15 feet just offshore at the reef line with breakers as high as 10 feet affected the Palm Beach County coast during the day on May 8th. This resulted in severe beach erosion and coastal flooding along most of the Palm Beach County coast. ||The most severe erosion and flooding took place on Jupiter Beach where a lifeguard storage building was destroyed and coastal streets were closed due to the flooding. Beaches and beachfront parks were closed. A 27-foot outboard boat capsized just north of Manalapan, with the 2 occupants able to swim safely to shore.||Moderate to severe beach erosion was reported at Juno Beach, South Palm Beach, Lantana, Delray Beach and Boca Raton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms affected the Naples area, producing hail, strong winds and minor flooding. About 1,000 customers were without power in Collier County as a result of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds flipped over a Cessna 150 plane which was tied down at the Naples Municipal Airport. Minor damage was also noted at the airport's commercial terminal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a fire at an apartment complex in Plantation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the roof of The Waves apartment complex in Plantation. The strike set off flames in the attic and severely damaged four apartments.||A total of 9 people were displaced from their homes because of the fires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail observed in Naples Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy and persistent rains along a stalled front over extreme southeast Florida caused significant flooding across portions of north and northeast Miami-Dade County. Areas affected include North Miami Beach, North Miami and Miami Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains caused roads to be closed in multiple locations across North Miami Beach, with numerous reports of water-filled cars stalled at underpasses. ||Media also reported a few businesses and apartments in North Miami and North Miami Beach with water intrusion.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding near 111 NE 58th Street in Miami and adjacent portion of North Miami Avenue. Street was impassable.

East winds of 15 to 20 mph persisted for several days and caused favorable conditions for numerous rip currents.||A total of 30 rescues were performed along Palm Beach County beaches alone, with many more likely to have occurred across all of southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32-year old man drowned trying to save a girl caught in a rip current at the beach behind the Four Seasons hotel in Palm Beach.  His friend, a 31-year-old man, was rescued and taken to the hospital.||No lifeguards are stationed at the beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three people drowned at southeast Florida beaches over the Memorial Day weekend as a result of rip currents. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18-year-old male drowned while swimming with two teenage friends along Miami Beach at 70th Street. No lifeguards were on duty at the time of the drowning around 7:30 PM EDT. The body washed ashore about 4 hours later about 5 blocks away.||The two friends were taken to the hospital and released shortly thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rains that created flash flooding in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains in a short period of time caused several roads in the southwest part of the county to flood.  The roads had to be closed for about an hour due to the high water.  Estimates of 5 to 6 inches of rain fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a late-season cold front and swept across large parts of South Florida during the evening hours of May 6th. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail damaged crops at two fields east of Belle Glade. Thousands of ears of corn suffered damage in 160 acres at Hundley Farms, and a 30 acre field of lettuce and other greens were damaged at Growers Management. Losses were estimated at about $750,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low took on Subtropical characteristics and became Subtropical Storm Andrea on May 9th at 10:00 EST.  High Surf and Beach Erosion were the primary impacts from the storm. The majority of damage was done while the storm was still a coastal low on May 7-9th. High Surf occurred from Fernandina Beach south to Flagler Beach with 6 to 8 foot breakers. Significant beach erosion along the St Johns and Flagler County beaches...but again most erosion occurred prior Andrea due to strong gradient flow from the coastal low that evolved into Andrea. The Flagler Beach Pier had to be closed due to high surf on May 8th and was re-opened late on the 9th. Minor to moderate beach erosion was reported around South 13th Street in Flagler Beach. The Florida Department of Transportation had to fill in areas near the seawall with sand. Ten homes in the 2800 Block of South Ponte Vedra Beach, St. Johns County were threatened with undermining due to beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Surf occurred from Fernandina Beach south to Flagler Beach with 6 to 8 foot breakers. Significant beach erosion along the St Johns and Flagler County beaches...but most erosion occurred prior Andrea due to strong gradient flow from the coastal low that evolved into Andrea. Ten homes in the 2800 Block of South Ponte Vedra Beach, St. Johns County were threatened with undermining due to beach erosion. The Flagler Beach Pier had to be closed due to high surf on May 8th and was re-opened on the 9th. Minor to moderate beach erosion was reported around South 13th Street in Flagler Beach. The Florida Department of Transportation had to fill in areas near the seawall with sand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire which broke out around 2 p.m. EDT on Tuesday May 1st near the Hunters Ridge Subdivision in southern Flagler County, Florida grew and continued for a large part of the month of May. On May 2nd at 3 p.m. EDT evacuations had to be ordered the Rima Ridge neighborhood as the southern side of the fire broke out and expanded towards that area.  Approximately 350 residents in 147 homes were evacuated from the area and the fire had grown to 1,200 acres.  A shelter was opened at Buddy Taylor Middle School for evacuees and was closed when evacuation order was lifted at approximately 7:16 p.n. EDT and residents were allowed to return to their homes.  By 1 p.m. EDT, May 3rd the fire had grown to 2,207 acres and 3,700 acres by 11:30 a.m. on may 4th.  By May 10th the fire was 50% contained and had burned 6,800 acres but was brought within control lines. The fire continued into June with some relief with the rains from Tropical Storm Barry (6/1-6/2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire which broke out around 2 p.m. EDT on Tuesday May 1st near the Hunters Ridge Subdivision in southern Flagler County, Florida grew and continued for a large part of the month of May. On May 2nd at 3 p.m. EDT evacuations had to be ordered the Rima Ridge neighborhood as the southern side of the fire broke out and expanded towards that area.  Approximately 350 residents in 147 homes were evacuated from the area and the fire had grown to 1,200 acres.  A shelter was opened at Buddy Taylor Middle School for evacuees and was closed when evacuation order was lifted at approximately 7:16 p.n. EDT and residents were allowed to return to their homes.  By 1 p.m. EDT, May 3rd the fire had grown to 2,207 acres and 3,700 acres by 11:30 a.m. on may 4th.  By May 10th the fire was 50% contained and had burned 6,575 (Florida Forestry) acres but was brought within control lines. The fire continued into June with some relief with the rains from Tropical Storm Barry (6/1-6/2). Fire was closed on July 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfire broke out on the afternoon of April 20th and rapidly spread due to low humidity and strong winds. The fire reached 1,000 acres and was contained on the 22nd. The Florida Division of Forestry closed out the fire on June 6th, but active fire spread cease on or about April 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wildfire broke out on the afternoon of April 20th and rapidly spread due to low humidity and strong winds. The fire reached 1,000 acres and was contained on the 22nd. The Florida Division of Forestry closed out the fire on June 6th, but active fire spread ceased on or about April 22nd.

Significant beach erosion along the St Johns and Flagler County beaches...but again most erosion occurred prior Andrea due to strong gradient flow from the coastal low that evolved into Andrea. The Flagler Beach Pier had to be closed due to high surf on May 8th and was re-opened late on the 9th. Minor to moderate beach erosion was reported around South 13th Street in Flagler Beach. The Florida Department of Transportation had to fill in areas near the seawall with sand. Ten homes in the 2800 Block of South Ponte Vedra Beach, St. Johns County were threatened with undermining due to beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Surf occurred from Fernandina Beach south to Flagler Beach with 6 to 8 foot breakers. Significant beach erosion along the St Johns and Flagler County beaches...but most erosion occurred prior Andrea due to strong gradient flow from the coastal low that evolved into Andrea. Ten homes in the 2800 Block of South Ponte Vedra Beach, St. Johns County were threatened with undermining due to beach erosion. The Flagler Beach Pier had to be closed due to high surf on May 8th and was re-opened on the 9th. Minor to moderate beach erosion was reported around South 13th Street in Flagler Beach. The Florida Department of Transportation had to fill in areas near the seawall with sand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fifty rip currents were reported in the waters off Walton County by the South Walton County Fire District during the afternoon of March 22.  These rip currents were directly responsible for the drownings of two men, one at Grayton Beach, and the other at Miramar Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walton County Sheriff officials reported that a man drowned while attempting to rescue four people who were in distress on a raft.  The four people on the raft made it safely to shore.  The man was trapped in a combination of a rip current and a long shore current running parallel to the beach.  The drowning occurred more than 800 yards from the nearest lifeguard tower at Miramar Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of hazardous conditions impacted the east-central Florida coast, primarily along the Volusia and Brevard County beaches, due to a combination of high surf and rip currents. A man drowned in the surf at Daytona Beach after being caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 19 year old male was knocked down by high surf then likely pulled seaward by a rip current at Daytona Beach. He was last seen struggling to stay afloat. His body was recovered the following day and autopsy results confirmed he died of drowning.

The damage amount includes $300 thousand to rebuild the home and $75 thousand to replace the contents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Barry made landfall near Tampa Bay as a tropical depression during the morning of June 2 with sporadic reports of heavy rain and strong winds across eastern portions of the Florida Big Bend.  The peak wind gust was 33 knots at Perry, FL.  The lowest sea level pressure was 1001 mb at Cross City, FL.  Storm rainfall totals ranged from one to seven inches.  Maximum storm tides were near a foot along the coasts of Taylor and Dixie counties.  The only damage reported was trees down throughout Madison County, FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall from Tropical Storm Barry ranged from three to five inches across the county.  Storm tides near a foot were reported along the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Non-convective wind gusts during the early afternoon of April 15, 2007, produced widespread tree and power line damage across portions of north Florida following the passage of a strong front and squall line earlier in the day. From noon through 1400 local time, numerous reports of downed street lights, power lines and trees were reported in Duval and Flagler counties. Wind gusts to 45 mph were measured at Flagler Beach around 1324 local time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts reported at 45 miles an hour at Flagler Beach at 13:24. around 14:00 a tree was blown down over U.S. Highway 1 at Belle Terrace Parkway in South Palm Coast. A second tree was blown down blocking the road at County Road  305 Daytona North. later in the day, a tree fell on house with minor roof damage at 77 Bainbridge Lane. At 68 Riverview Drive a tree on house with roof damage and at 95 Ratlisse Road a tree was blown down on a house and fence with minor damage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring across South Florida combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006 produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal winter and spring rainfall along with a prolonged period of dry weather going back to early 2006 caused for severe drought conditions to spread southward across South Florida. As of May 29th, all of South Florida north of a West Palm Beach to Naples line was under D3 (extreme) drought status, with areas south of that line in a D2 (severe) drought status, with the exception of Miami-Dade County which was not considered to be in a drought.||Rainfall had begun to fall a little more regularly by the end of the month across eastern sections, but remained sporadic all month across interior and western areas.||The level of Lake Okeechobee continued to lower, and reached a all-time record low of 8.94 feet by May 31st. Significant impacts continued for the general public as water usage restrictions continued across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in effect while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across most of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with scattered thunderstorms early in the month resulted in several wildfires breaking out across South Florida as a result of lighning. One wildfire burned in the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County for over a month from a lightning strike on May 4th, with over 60,000 acres burned. Another wildfire in the Picayune Strand State Forest started from lightning on May 7th, burning over 18,000 acres. On May 8th, the fire damaged 3 homes in the Golden Gate Estates area and threatened 33 others. Yet another lightning-sparked wildfire in Hendry County started on May 19th, consuming over 4,000 acres and threatening homes in Felda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall since early 2006 produced drought conditions across the Lake Okeechobee region of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal rainfall since early 2006 produced severe drought conditions across the Lake Okeechobee region of South Florida. Large portions of Glades, Hendry and northern Palm Beach counties were under D2 (severe) drought status. The level of Lake Okeechobee lowered to 10.55 feet, about 3 feet below normal and the lowest  level since May 2001. Impacts were primarily to agriculture, with 90 percent of the area's topsoil moisture rated very short to short, and 80 percent of pastures rated very poor to poor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Barry began as a westward moving tropical wave that created a broad area of low pressure near the eastern coast of the Yucatan Peninsula on May 30th.  The convection associated with the wave became more organized and a tropical depression formed near the western tip of Cuba during the morning of June 1st then became a tropical storm that afternoon. The storm reached it's peak of 50 knots that evening before weakening due to strong wind shear associated with an upper level trough over the northern Gulf of Mexico. Tropical Storm Barry moved northeast and made landfall in the Tampa Bay area around 10 AM on June 2nd with 34 kt to 45 kt winds. ||In Pinellas County the peak wind at the fire station on Treasure Island was 34 knots.  The peak wind at Albert Whitted Airport was 28 knots 4:36 PM EST and the peak wind at the St Petersburg/Clearwater International Airport was 32 knots at 2:01 PM EST. A rip current caused one fatality.  An unconscious 45-year-old female was found in heavy surf near 178th Avenue in Indian Shores. Fire department rescue did establish a weak pulse but she died four days later on June 6th. Power outages affected about 78,000 customers.  People had to canoe across roads in the flood-prone areas of the Shore Acres neighborhood in St. Petersburg. The tidal gauge on the pier at Clearwater Beach recorded a 1.91 foot surge and a 4.78 foot storm tide, both relative to Mean Low Low Water. ||In Hillsborough County the peak wind at MacDill Air Force Base was 39 knots at 801 AM EST.  Wind gusts estimated at 45 knots knocked a centruy-old oak onto a four bedroom house in Carrollwood and caused one injury to a 4-year-old girl while she was sleeping. ||In Manatee County the peak wind at the the Bradenton/Sarasota International Airport was 34 knots at 9 AM EST. ||In Sarasota County the peak wind at the Venice C-MAN station was 36 knots at 7 AM EST. ||In Polk County the peak wind at the Winter Haven Airport was 36 knots at 325 PM EST and the peak wind at the Bartow Airport was 34 knots at 350 PM EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tidal gauge on the pier at Clearwater Beach recorded a 1.91 foot surge and a 4.78 foot storm tide, both relative to Mean Low Low Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface pressure ridge axis extended across southern Florida and the west coast sea breeze front was most active from Tampa through Fort Myers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of penny to nickel size hail from Sebring to Lake Placid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry spring across South Florida combined with a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006 produced severe to extreme drought conditions across most of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal winter and spring rainfall along with a prolonged period of dry weather going back to early 2006 caused for severe drought conditions to spread southward across South Florida. As of May 29th, all of South Florida north of a West Palm Beach to Naples line was under D3 (extreme) drought status, with areas south of that line in a D2 (severe) drought status, with the exception of Miami-Dade County which was not considered to be in a drought.||Rainfall had begun to fall a little more regularly by the end of the month across eastern sections, but remained sporadic all month across interior and western areas.||The level of Lake Okeechobee continued to lower, and reached a all-time record low of 8.94 feet by May 31st. Significant impacts continued for the general public as water usage restrictions continued across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in effect while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across most of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal, and underground reservoirs were also running below normal area-wide.||The extremely dry conditions along with scattered thunderstorms early in the month resulted in several wildfires breaking out across South Florida as a result of lighning. One wildfire burned in the Big Cypress National Preserve in Collier County for over a month from a lightning strike on May 4th, with over 60,000 acres burned. Another wildfire in the Picayune Strand State Forest started from lightning on May 7th, burning over 18,000 acres. On May 8th, the fire damaged 3 homes in the Golden Gate Estates area and threatened 33 others. Yet another lightning-sparked wildfire in Hendry County started on May 19th, consuming over 4,000 acres and threatening homes in Felda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Florida west coast and ease coast sea breezes collided over central Florida producing widespread hail, wind damage and funnel clouds.

An un-seasonal amplified upper level trough and attendant surface front moved into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong differential positive vorticity advection, high surface based instability, large CAPE in the hail growth zone and low level forcing spawned widespread severe storms. Aloft cool 500 mb around -10 degrees Celsius were noted and mid level dry air moved over the area, and both factors increased downdraft strength in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst occurred over Orange Park and Fleming Island around 530 pm local time. At 530 pm, a former NWS employee in Orange Park reported a wall cloud with strong rotation. The cloud snapped tree tops in the neighborhood. A roof was also blown off a home on Arbor Circle Drive. By 532 pm, numerous trees were down in Doctors Inlet and Fleming Island. Trees were blown down across the CSX railroad tracks in Doctors Inlet and a gas station also suffered tree damage. At 540 pm, winds were gusting around 50 mph and pea size hail was reported on Fleming Island. Wind gusts snapped a pine tree which fell and damaged a screened room in Eagle Harbor. By 545 pm, widespread tree damage occurred on the southern tier of Fleming Island, south of Hibernia and north of Black Creek. Tree tops were snapped and blown off in Fleming Island Plantation, and some were blocking roads in the subdivision. Minor damage occurred to a mobile home on Floyd Street and a cabinet shop off of Ebb Tide Road and U.S. Highway 17 had roof damage. A portion of the baseball backstop at St. Johns Country Day School on Doctors Lake Drive was bent and several large trees on campus were snapped or uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in the 1500 block of NW 167th Avenue in Pembroke Pines. The attic was filled with smoke and a vent on the home was charred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough moving southward across Florida on July 21st destabilized the atmosphere and set the state for severe afternoon thunderstorms across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The landmark Casa Amado mansion at 455 North County Road which was undergoing renovations was severely damaged by an apparent lightning strike. The mansion was totaled, with damage estimated at $15 million or more. ||The fire department was called in at 1135 PM after calls reporting smoke were received. Storms affected the area sometime around 230 PM, which is the time the lightning strike was believed to have occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough moving southward across Florida on July 21st destabilized the atmosphere and set the state for severe afternoon thunderstorms across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch of 6 to 8 inches thick was broken off tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions which began during the spring months lingered into the summer across interior portions of South Florida, as a result of a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions which began during the spring months lingered through July across portions of South Florida due to a prolonged period of below normal rainfall going back to early 2006. D2 (severe) drought conditions continued across Glades, Hendry and Western Palm Beach counties. ||Lake Okeechobee set a record low level on June 30th of 8.88 feet, and July began near 8.9 feet. Despite near normal rainfall across the region, the lake only rose slightly to about 9.2 feet by the end of July. ||This slow rise in the lake level resulted in a continuation of significant drought impacts for the general public, with water usage restrictions continuing across South Florida. Phase 3 restrictions (50 percent reduction in usage) were in place for the Lake Okeechobee area, while the rest of South Florida was under Phase 2 restrictions (30 percent reduction). Wells across interior and western sections of South Florida were running at below 10 percent of normal. Underground reservoirs returned to near normal levels.||Several small wildfires broke out across South Florida during July, with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout and at least two tornadoes formed along the west Florida coast sea breeze front in the Fort Myers area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore near Estero Boulevard and Canal Street as a tornado and damaged the roof of a resort hotel and a nearby Tiki bar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather continued over parts of South Florida on July 23rd as a mid to upper level trough continued over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broward County Sheriff's Office reported golf ball size hail along Nob Hill Road between Oakland Park Blvd and NW 33rd Street in Sunrise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 20-year old man in Boca Raton during the afternoon of July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20-year-old male was struck while lightning while playing soccer at Lake Wyman Park in Boca Raton. The victim went to the hospital and died 3 days later. Time of lightning strike is estimated.||A thunderstorm had passed through the park and the victim along with a group of friends took refuge in a park restroom. After the rain stopped, the group went back outside. Lightning struck shortly thereafter, striking the ground near the victim and traveled along the wet grass, entering the victim's left foot. There was a hole in his left shoe and both his socks were burned off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of South Florida during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 to 70 mph by trained spotter on Deer Run Road near Lion Country Safari. Downed trees and pea sized hail were reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms produced damaging winds, hail and lightning to portions of Leon, Calhoun and Wakulla counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in the 5000 block of Shady View Lane.  The fire spread in the the attic and through the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced two day total rainfall amounts of 15 to 20 inches with isolated higher amounts along the coastal sections of the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets in the south part of the county had to be closed for several hours due to high water.  The flooded streets were generally along and south of Interstate 10.  Water approached several houses, but never entered any homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere combined with outflow boundaries and a weak upper level disturbance to produce scattered thunderstorms in west central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea and a few half dollar size hail was reported along Bloomingdale Avenue  east of Highway 301.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along the west coast sea breeze front late in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53-year-old man was struck by lightning while standing on a pier on the Caloosahatchee River near Freemont Street. He was taken to the hospital and died the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth consecutive day, strong afternoon thunderstorms affected Southeast Florida. The strongest storms affected Miami-Dade County with flooding and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in South Miami and The Falls from very heavy rains. A former NWS employee measured 6 inches of rain in a little over 3 hours in South Miami. Street flooding was reported at the intersection of SW 40th Street and SW 67th Avenue in South Miami, as well as along SW 136th Street near The Falls Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth consecutive day, strong afternoon thunderstorms affected Southeast Florida. The strongest storms affected Miami-Dade County with flooding and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home on SW 13th Street in the Little Gables area was struck by |lightning. The bolt struck a tree before hitting the house, scattering pieces of the roof across the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from afternoon thunderstorms struck a man in Miami Lakes on July 8th. The man died 5 days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40-year-old man was struck by lightning while walking door to door along NW 199th Street in Miami Lakes. The man was taken to the Ryder Trauma Center where he died 5 days later.||Light rain was falling at the time of the lightning strike, although there were no dark clouds directly overhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected parts of South Florida during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three men were struck by lightning while moving furniture in LaBelle. They were taken to a nearby hospital as a precaution. One of the victims was knocked unconscious for a few minutes, then complained of pain in his chest and throat after regaining consciousness.  Time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on July 4th contained frequent lightning which caused damage to several structures. About 10,000 customers lost power in Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three homes were struck by lightning in Boynton Beach during a 48-minute period on July 4th. The area affected was between Congress Avenue and Jog Road. ||One home in the 12300 block of Forest Greens Drive suffered about $100,000 in fire and smoke damage to the attic. A second home in the 9700 block of Coronado Lakes Drive suffered about $25,000 in damage when a tower atop the home was struck, with tiles being torn off the roof. ||The third home suffered the most damaged, estimated at $750,000, in the 4100 block of Juniper Terrace. Fire started in the attic and eventually collapsed a portion of the roof. Sixty percent of the roof burned away.||Over 600 lightning strikes were recorded in Palm Beach County between 3 and 5 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms on July 4th contained frequent lightning which caused damage to several structures. About 10,000 customers lost power in Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in The Redland, displacing a family of eight. The lightning bolt struck a corner of the house and started a fire in the attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough moving southward across Florida on July 21st destabilized the atmosphere and set the state for severe afternoon thunderstorms across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Beach County Sheriff's Office reported a funnel cloud at Palmetto Park and Powerline Road in Boca Raton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level trough moving southward across Florida on July 21st destabilized the atmosphere and set the state for severe afternoon thunderstorms across Southeast Florida.
A mid and upper level trough moving southward across Florida on July 21st destabilized the atmosphere and set the state for severe afternoon thunderstorms across Southeast Florida.
A mid and upper level trough moving southward across Florida on July 21st destabilized the atmosphere and set the state for severe afternoon thunderstorms across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained storm spotter reported a tornado about 4 miles west of the intersection of Krome Avenue and Kendall Drive in unpopulated Miami-Dade County. Law enforcement confirmed the tornado, which did no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced excessive lighting in the coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a house fire in Palm Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse thunderstorms on the coast produced hail in Vero Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported along US Highway 1 just north of Vero Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced two day total rainfall amounts of 15 to 20 inches with isolated higher amounts along the coastal sections of the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets in the south part of the county had to be closed for several hours due to high water.  The flooded streets were generally along and south of Highway 90.  Water approached several houses, but never entered any homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado moved across Pensacola, just missing the downtown area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey was conducted Thursday afternoon on the tornado that struck Pensacola Florida on October 18.  The tornado was rated an EF1 on the new enhanced Fujita scale (86-109 mph).  The maximum width of the tornado was about 150 yards and the track length was about 5.5 miles. The tornado began around 1010 am CDT and lasted for about fifteen minutes.|The storm probably started as a waterspout over the Bay. Damage first began along the water just northeast of Pensacola Naval Air Station (just south of Silvershore drive).  Minor damage occurred in this area.  The storm tracked northeast over the water just parallel to the shore and moved ashore near the old Bayou Chico drawbridge.  Several homes in this area suffered roof damage.  Several boats were flipped over in this area as there are several boat storage facilities located near the water.|The tornado moved northeast and crossed Garden street near E street. Minor tree and power line damage occurred along this track. The tornado started causing a little more damage near Gregory and D streets as this is a more populated area.  The tornado continued tracking northeast and crossed Cervantes near A street and continued northeast and crossed Highway 29 near Mallory street.  Most of the home and church damage occurred along the track from B to Barcelona streets.  A couple of homes near Jackson street were heavily damaged and a church on A street had part of its roof taken off.  Four minor injuries also happened in this area.  A daycare center in the church had been alerted to a tornado and had taken cover several minutes before the tornado hit.|After crossing Highway 29 the storm crossed I-110 just north of the pool and started tracking a little more to the north northeast and crossed Davis near the ball fields.  Mostly tree and power line damage occurred along this track.  The ball fields had bleachers turned over and power lines down.|The storm continued to the north northeast and crossed Ninth avenue near Texar drive and crossed Bayou boulevard near the Target store and eventually dissipated near Cordova mall.  Most of the damage from the ball fields to Target was tree damage and minor roof damage.  Thirty cars in the Target parking lot were totally destroyed.  Very little damage occurred to the store.  Across the street, Office Depot suffered roof damage.  Part of the mall had skylights damaged and this allowed water to fall into part of the mall.  No damage was found north of the mall.|The storm destroyed four structures...caused major damage to twenty-four structures and caused minor damage to fifty eight structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough and surface cold front spawned severe weather across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sheriff reported a tornado moving along the marshes of the intracoastal waterway near Matanzas Inlet. The tornado tracked across southern Anastasia Island before exiting offshore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level trough over the eastern United States and diffuse surface cold front sparked storms over the forecast area. Convergent bands of convections moved onshore during the late morning hours, and one cell developed a waterspout offshore of Flagler Beach. The cell moved onshore, as did the waterspout.  The EF0 tornado damaged parts of Flagler Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore as a tornado around 1254 pm local time in Flagler Beach. The spout moved west-southwest and crossed over the Flagler Beach pier around 1255 pm. The spout made landfall at A1A and South 2nd street and moved west tearing awnings and roofing materials. Debris was hung up in power line wires. The tornado moved west along South 2nd Street and Flagler Avenue where it damaged a large awning before the tornado lifted into the parent storm. The tornado moved about 3 blocks inland before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front just north of the Altamaha River, the sea breezes, surface based instability and upper level positive differential vorticity advection all merged to spawn scattered severe storms across our forecast area in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Military observers reported a brief tornado touchdown on base. The vortex was on the ground for around 2 minutes. It blew out windows from a three story barracks building and blew around heavy equipment. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced two day total rainfall amounts of 15 to 20 inches with isolated higher amounts along the coastal sections of the counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets in the south part of the county had to be closed for several hours due to high water.  The flooded streets were generally along and south of Interstate 10.  Water approached several houses, but never entered any homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men drowned from rip currents at Miramar Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven swimmers were rescued from rip currents at Miramar Beach by the beach patrol.  Five were rushed to a local hospital.  Two of the victims died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term rainfall deficits continue to result in drought conditions over portions of interior and southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted over interior and southwest sections of South Florida as long term rainfall deficits and relative lack of summer rainfall continue. The level of Lake Okeechobee continued to rise only modestly, with lake levels remaining well below 10 feet, 5 feet below normal. Increasing rains by the second week of August were sufficient enough to downgrade the drought status from severe to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Southeast Florida due to the interaction of a surface trough across the area and northerly flow aloft associated with a weak mid to upper level low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported in Coral Gables.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Southeast Florida due to the interaction of a surface trough across the area and northerly flow aloft associated with a weak mid to upper level low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail in Miami Shores in the vicinity of North Miami Avenue and 106 Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Southeast Florida due to the interaction of a surface trough across the area and northerly flow aloft associated with a weak mid to upper level low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail reported along Palmetto Expressway (SR 826) and NW 68th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Southeast Florida due to the interaction of a surface trough across the area and northerly flow aloft associated with a weak mid to upper level low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 54-year-old man was struck and injured by lightning while leaning against a crane outside a business. Lightning apparently struck the crane and the man felt tingling in his left arm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across Southeast Florida due to the interaction of a surface trough across the area and northerly flow aloft associated with a weak mid to upper level low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coral Gables Police reported a house fire off Old Cutler Road caused by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A life guard supervisor reports four miles of major beach erosion has occurred in three separate areas along St Johns County beaches. The areas are south of the gate station in South Ponte Vedra Beach...at St Augustine Beach near the pier...and near Marineland. Also...low lying areas are flooding during high tides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge built north of the region with an elongated fetch area set up from near Bermuda to north Florida. A trough and eventually a low pressure center developed to the south of the region over the Bahamas and south Florida with a strong gradient flow between the two from September 29th through October 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A life guard supervisor reported unusually high tide with water coming over the berms and into low lying streets. Low tide was at 7 am EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event lasted into the first few days of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf began to impact the Palm Beach County coast on October 29th, with water levels rising sufficiently enough to cause severe beach erosion and coastal flooding beginning on October 30th and continuing through October 31st and into the first few days of November. Extreme beach erosion was observed along the central and northern Palm Beach coast from Lantana north to Jupiter. In some areas, the dune line was completely eroded, with oceanfront buildings sitting on top of 15 foot cliffs straight down to the water. A sea wall at the Imperial House condominiums in South Palm Beach collapsed from the pounding surf, and the east portion of the building was evacuated. South of Lantana to Boca Raton, erosion was reported as moderate to severe.||Damage estimates are included in November 2007 Storm Data.||Maximum storm tide observed at Virginia Key NOS tide gauge was 3.5 feet just before midnight on November 1, and the maximum surge height at that time was 1.08 feet. No tide measurements are available from Palm Beach County, but storm tide is estimated to have been as high as 2-3 feet over northern Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pulse thunderstorms produced lightning over central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school crossing guard was struck and injured by lightning while on duty in Winter Park. He was hospitalized with burns and released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event lasted into the first few days of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Noel moved north from eastern Cuba to across the western Bahama Islands from October 30 through October 31. The interaction of Noel with a strong high pressure area located over the mid-Atlantic states produced strong winds over southeast Florida and the adjacent waters well before Noel made its closest passage to the area early on November 1. Although the winds on October 30 and 31 which preceded the direct circulation of Noel were not directly associated with Noel, they are included under this Tropical Storm event category for the sake of simplicity. ||Highest sustained winds at ASOS sites ranged from 23 to 27 knots (27 to 31 mph), with highest gusts from 32 to 38 knots (37 to 44 mph). Over the coastal waters and along the immediate coast, sustained winds were estimated at around 30 to 33 knots (35 to 38 knots), with gusts near 40 knots (46 mph). ||Damage was minor and mainly confined to a few downed power lines. Around 5,000 customers lost power in the three-county area of Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade counties.   ||Rainfall amounts were rather light, ranging from a half-inch (0.5) to nearly two inches (1.94) 9 miles west of Sweetwater.||Lowest sea level pressures were in the 1003 to 1004 mb range.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a few trees and produced hail and some street flooding in Orlando.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms lingered over Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 9 inches of rain fell in a 24-hour period across parts of coastal Volusia County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime pulse thunderstorms produced hail, wind damage, lightning and funnel clouds over central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a funnel cloud over Ft. Pierce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 10 came ashore just southwest of Crestview, Florida during the evening of September 21.  The lowest sea level pressure recorded was 1005.3 mb at Buoy 42039, 80 nautical miles south of Panama City, Florida.  Both the maximum sustained wind and peak wind gust of 27 and 35 knots, respectively, were also recorded at the buoy.  Storm total rainfall ranged from 0.15 inches at Chipley to 2.37 inches at Apalachicola, Florida.  Storm tides ranged from 2.5 to 4.1 feet along the Panhandle coast.  There were no reports of beach erosion or inland flooding.  There were a few reports of funnel clouds in portions of the Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported north of Interstate 10 near the Walmart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the Mid-Atlantic states and Tropical Storm Noel over Hispaniola and eastern Cuba caused a prolonged period of strong east winds over Southeast Florida and the adjacent waters. As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event lasted into the first few days of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Noel moved north from eastern Cuba to across the western Bahama Islands from October 30 through October 31. The interaction of Noel with a strong high pressure area located over the mid-Atlantic states produced strong winds over southeast Florida and the adjacent waters well before Noel made its closest passage to the area early on November 1. Although the winds on October 30 and 31 which preceded the direct circulation of Noel were not directly associated with Noel, they are included under this Tropical Storm event category for the sake of simplicity. ||Highest sustained winds at ASOS sites ranged from 23 to 27 knots (27 to 31 mph), with highest gusts from 32 to 38 knots (37 to 44 mph). Over the coastal waters and along the immediate coast, sustained winds were estimated at around 30 to 33 knots (35 to 38 knots), with gusts near 40 knots (46 mph). ||Damage was minor and mainly confined to a few downed power lines. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime pulse thunderstorms produced hail, wind damage, lightning and funnel clouds over central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small boat was struck by lightning in the Halifax River. The two people on board were transported to a local hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong pressure gradient between a weak low pressure area over the western Bahamas and strong high pressure over the Northeast United States, along with deep moisture and instability east of an upper level low west of Naples, led to the formation of fast moving showers and thunderstorms which produced locally strong winds across Southeast Florida. Severe thunderstorms were observed over Southeast Miami-Dade County, and strong non-thunderstorm winds caused beach erosion in Palm Beach County.||This is a continuation of the episode which began on September 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf with waves of 10-12 feet pounded the Palm Beach County shoreline from September 30 through October 2. Major beach erosion was observed on Jupiter Beach, Singer Island, Lantana and Delray Beach. About 12 feet of beach was lost at Lantana and Delray Beach which eroded the dune line. A portion of a beachfront condominium retaining wall collapsed in Lantana. A lifeguard stand was washed away in Delray Beach, and small oceanfront buildings were threatened at Jupiter Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions prevailed over most of interior and southwest Florida during October as near to below normal monthly rain, along with long term rainfall deficits, have kept the region very dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly below normal rain during October, along with long term rainfall deficits, resulted in the continuing of severe drought conditions for interior South Florida, including the Lake Okeechobee region. The level of Lake Okeechobee during October was between 10 feet and 10.40 feet, which set record low daily levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Tropical Depression number 10 began to slowly organize in the Gulf of Mexico, low level rotation allowed numerous strong mesocyclones to track north over western Orange and northern Lake counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the Town of Eustis at the northern end of West Crooked Lake. The tornado quickly intensified to EF1 strength as it moved north northwest through Eustis. The track was just under two miles long and had a maximum width of 300 yards.  The tornado destroyed 7 homes. Twenty seven homes received major damage and 81 homes reported minor damage. There were no serious injuries and property damage was set at $6.2 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted during afternoon thunderstorms just west of Boca Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near South County Regional Park in West Boca. The funnel was likely in the vicinity of Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moving south across Miami-Dade County produced a lightning strike which injured 3 people in Coral Gables.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three roofers working atop a home in Coral Gables were struck by lightning. The three men were knocked unconscious by the lightning bolt, but regained consciousness by the time they were transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large surf produced rip currents along the east central Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14 year old boy went into rough surf to help a friend keep her footing and drowned in a rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term rainfall deficits along with recent lack of rain led to the continuation of drought conditions to much of Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rain during the first half of September, along with long term rainfall deficits, resulted in the continuation of severe drought conditions for much of Collier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term rainfall deficits along with recent lack of rain led to the continuation of drought conditions to much of Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rain during the first half of September, along with long term rainfall deficits, resulted in the continuation of severe drought conditions for much of Collier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developing along sea breeze boundaries over interior South Florida produced a brief tornado in the Big Cypress Preserve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports received by Collier County EOC by motorists along Alligator Alley of a brief tornado touchdown near the intersection of Alligator Alley (I-75) and State Road 29. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong pressure gradient between a weak low pressure area over the western Bahamas and strong high pressure over the Northeast United States, along with deep moisture and instability east of an upper level low west of Naples, led to the formation of fast moving showers and thunderstorms which produced locally strong winds across Southeast Florida. Severe thunderstorms were observed over Southeast Miami-Dade County, and strong non-thunderstorm winds caused beach erosion in Palm Beach County.||This is a continuation of the episode which began on September 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall spotter in the Saga Bay section of Cutler Bay recorded a 68 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and dry high pressure built across the region behind a strong cold front. Strong cold air advection through the day and radiational cooling under clear skies and calm winds that night allowed several locations across the northwest Florida peninsula to fall below freezing for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures were generally in the upper 20s across the county, with the cooperative station 3 miles southeast of Inverness reaching 29 degrees. The lowest temperature recorded was 26 degrees at Holder. The average duration of below freezing temperatures was 4 hours, with up to 8 hours in isolated locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado briefly touched down five miles west of Mayo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado uprooted large oak trees with debris on State Highway 53 and County Road 351.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime pulse thunderstorms produced hail, wind damage, lightning and funnel clouds over central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed in downtown Orlando.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A non-tropical area of low pressure in the central Gulf of Mexico caused tropical moisture to surge into Florida. The moisture interacted with a stationary front across central Florida to cause isolated areas of heavy rain due to storm redevelopment over the same areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.79 inches was measured at the USGS river gaging station. Radar estimated rainfall of 6 to 8 inches from Fort Lonesome to Duette in Manatee County. The excess water filled ditches but no homes received damage due to rising water. The Little manatee River 4.2 miles southwest of Wimauma rose 5 feet between 8 PM EDT to 2 AM EDT with a maximum stage of 11.97 feet at 2 PM on October 3rd. Flood stage is 11 feet and only minor flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A non-tropical area of low pressure in the central Gulf of Mexico caused tropical moisture to surge into Florida. The moisture interacted with a stationary front across central Florida to cause isolated areas of heavy rain due to storm redevelopment over the same areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 7.07 inches was measured at the USGS river gauge. The rain resulted in rapid rise of more than 9 feet in four hours of the Manatee River 8 miles west of Myakka Head. The flood stage is 11 feet and the crest was 15.82 feet at 8 AM EDT on October 3rd. Releases from the Manatee Dam began as a result, and the excess water flooded the Rye Bridge downstream. Only minor flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed as a trough of low pressure in place across central Florida combined with upper level energy passing over the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On a construction site near Brandon, a 52-year-old man sitting in the cab of a crane was injured when lightning struck the crane's boom. The man suffered burns to his face and was unable to see out of his right eye.

As Noel moved north across the western Bahamas, the strong winds continued across southeast Florida. The event caused severe beach erosion, coastal flooding, and minor wind damage. The event lasted into the first few days of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf began to impact the Broward County coast on October 29th, with water levels rising sufficiently enough to cause moderate to isolated pockets of severe beach erosion and coastal flooding beginning on October 30th, and continuing through October 31st and into the first few days of November. Areas most affected by the beach erosion were Fort Lauderdale Beach and Hollywood Beach. Sand had to be removed Highway AIA in Fort Lauderdale Beach, and estimates of only 10 remaining yards of beach were received from Hollywood Beach.||Maximum storm tide observed at Virginia Key NOS tide gauge was 3.5 feet just before midnight on November 1, and the maximum surge height at that time was 1.08 feet. No tide measurements are available from Broward County, but storm tide is estimated to have been as high as 1-2 feet over portions of Broward County.

The event lasted into the first few days of November.
